ref: In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.
text: Mpiin na Uwumbɔr nan naan kitiŋ,

ref: The earth was barren, with no form of life; it was under a roaring ocean covered with darkness. But the Spirit of God was moving over the water. The First Day
text: le kitiŋ aa nan kpa liker. Nnyun, ni mbɔmbɔɔn ŋmeen le nan bi ni kookoo. Uwumbɔr Aafuur Nyaan mu le nan bi nnyun ngbaan aapaacham.

ref: God said, “I command light to shine!” And light started shining.
text: Le Uwumbɔr bui ke, “Nwiihn ŋa,” le mu ŋa.

ref: God looked at the light and saw that it was good. He separated light from darkness
text: U nan kan ke nwiihn ngbaan ŋan, le u yakr nwiihn ni mbɔmbɔɔn,

ref: and named the light “Day” and the darkness “Night.” Evening came, then morning—that was the first day. The Second Day
text: ki yin nwiihn ke “Nwiin pu,” ki yin mbɔmbɔɔn ke “Kinyeek,” le ki nan joor ki woln kichakpiik ni. Njan aataak daal le na.

ref: and named it “Sky.” Evening came, then morning—that was the second day. The Third Day
text: le Uwumbɔr yin nima ke “Kitaapaak.” Le ni ki joor ki ki woln kichakpiik ni. Winlee daal le na.

ref: God said, “I command the water under the sky to come together in one place, so there will be dry ground.” And that's what happened.
text: Uwumbɔr nan ki bui ke, “Nnyun mu bi kitiŋ pu na kpaan tɔb chee ki cha kitiŋ.” Le mu ŋa kina.

ref: God named the dry ground “Land,” and he named the water “Ocean.” God looked at what he had done and saw that it was good.
text: Uwumbɔr nan yin nnyun aah kaa bi nin chee na ke “Kitiŋ,” ki yin mu aah kuun doon nin chee na ke “Nnyusakpem.” U nan kan ke ni ŋan.

ref: God said, “I command the earth to produce all kinds of plants, including fruit trees and grain.” And that's what happened.
text: Le u bui ke, “Tisufar aabɔŋ ti lu njikaabim na, ni isui i lu ŋisubil na, ni tijikaar mɔk aabɔŋ, punn ni.” Le ni ŋa kina.

ref: The earth produced all kinds of vegetation. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Le tisufar ni isui mɔmɔk lu ŋisubil ni tijikaar mɔk aabɔŋ ki moo pɔr kpee. U nan kan ke ni ŋan.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the third day. The Fourth Day
text: Le ni ki joor ki ki woln kichakpiik ni. Wintaa daal le na.

ref: God said, “I command lights to appear in the sky and to separate day from night and to show the time for seasons, special days, and years.
text: Uwumbɔr nan ki bui ke, “Iwiihn nya ki li bi kitaapaak aan ki yakr nwiin pu ni kinyeek. Cha i yakr n‑yoonn, kitaak ki ti saa kitaak, ni njim ki ti saa njim, ni libiln ki ti saa libiln.

ref: I command them to shine on the earth.” And that's what happened.
text: Iwiihn ngbaan li wiin kitaapaak ki woln kitiŋ pu.” Le ni ŋa kina.

ref: God made two powerful lights, the brighter one to rule the day and the other to rule the night. He also made the stars.
text: Uwumbɔr nan naan iwiin sakpiin ilee la, nwiin li wiin nwiin pu, uŋmal li wiin kinyeek; u nan naan iŋmabi mu.

ref: Then God put these lights in the sky to shine on the earth,
text: Uwumbɔr nan di i ŋa kitaapaak aan i li wiin kitiŋ pu.

ref: to rule day and night, and to separate light from darkness. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: I li bi nwiin pu ni kinyeek, ki yakr nwiihn ni mbɔmbɔɔn tɔb chee. U nan kan ke ni ŋan.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the fourth day. The Fifth Day
text: Le ni ki joor ki ki woln kichakpiik ni. Winaa daal le na.

ref: God said, “I command the ocean to be full of living creatures, and I command birds to fly above the earth.”
text: Uwumbɔr nan ki bui ke, “Ijan mɔk aabɔŋ, ni tiwanfuurkaan mɔmɔk aabɔŋ naan, ki li bi nnyun ni ki li wiir. Inyoon mu mɔmɔk aabɔŋ li bi ki laani paacham.”

ref: So God made the giant sea monsters and all the living creatures that swim in the ocean. He also made every kind of bird. God looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Uwumbɔr nan naan ijasakpiin, ni iwaatiir, ki kpee tiwanfuurkaan mɔk aabɔŋ ki di ŋa nnyun ponn ni, ki naan inyoon aabɔŋ i li laani paacham. U nan kan ke ni ŋan.

ref: Then he gave the living creatures his blessing—he told the ocean creatures to increase and live everywhere in the ocean and the birds to increase everywhere on earth.
text: U nan ŋa tinyoor ŋa ni pu, ki bui ke nnyun ni aawan maa, paacham mu aawan too, ki li moo pɔr kpae.

ref: Evening came, then morning—that was the fifth day. The Sixth Day
text: Le ni ki joor ki ki woln kichakpiik ni. Winŋmu daal le na.

ref: God said, “I command the earth to give life to all kinds of tame animals, wild animals, and reptiles.” And that's what happened.
text: Uwumbɔr nan ki bui ke, “Tiwakor, ni ipeel, ni tiwan ni mɔmɔk baar na, ni ye nisakpiin, ni niwaatiir na, naan ki li bi kitiŋ pu.” Le ni ŋa kina.

ref: God made every one of them. Then he looked at what he had done, and it was good.
text: Uwumbɔr nan naan tiwan ngbaan mɔmɔk, le waah nan kan nimɔk na, ni ŋan u chee.

ref: God said, “Now we will make humans, and they will be like us. We will let them rule the fish, the birds, and all other living creatures.”
text: Uwumbɔr nan ki bui ke, “Cha ti naan binib, bi li bi ke timi na, ki li naahn timi. M ga tii bi mpɔɔn, bi li lik ijan, ni inyoon, ni ipeel, ni tiwakor, tisakpiin ni tiwaatiir mɔmɔk pu.”

ref: So God created humans to be like himself; he made men and women.
text: Uwumbɔr nan naan binib ki di naŋ ubaa. U nan naan uja ni upii la,

ref: God gave them his blessing and said: Have a lot of children! Fill the earth with people and bring it under your control. Rule over the fish in the ocean, the birds in the sky, and every animal on the earth.
text: le ki ŋa tinyoor ŋa bi pu, le ki bui ke, “Maa, ki pɔr, aan ki gbiin kitiŋ kee pu, ki li lik kitiŋ ngbaan. Li kpa mpɔɔn ijan, ni inyoon, ni tiwan nimɔk fu ki baar kitiŋ pu na.”

ref: I have provided all kinds of fruit and grain for you to eat.
text: Uwumbɔr nan ki bui ke, “M di njikaabim, ni isui i lu ŋisubil na mɔmɔk ki di tii nimi, ni li ji,

ref: And I have given the green plants as food for everything else that breathes, including animals, both wild and tame, and birds. And so it was.
text: ki di mmobim ni tisufar di tii ipeel, ni tiwakor, ni tiwan nimɔk fu ki baar kitiŋ na, ni inyoon, ni li ji ki li ŋmɔ.” Le ni ŋa kina.

ref: God looked at what he had done. All of it was very good! Evening came, then morning—that was the sixth day.
text: Le Uwumbɔr nan lik ki kan ke waah ŋa tiwan nimɔk na ŋan pam. Le ni ki joor, ki ki woln kichakpiik ni. Winloob daal le na.

ref: So the heavens and the earth and everything else were created. The Seventh Day
text: Kina le Uwumbɔr nan naan kitaapaak, ni kitiŋ, ni tiwan mɔmɔk doo.

ref: By the seventh day God had finished his work, and so he rested.
text: Winlole daal, le Uwumbɔr nan tun lituln mɔmɔk doo, ki kal fuur.

ref: God blessed the seventh day and made it special, because on that day he rested from his work.
text: Uwumbɔr nan ŋa tinyoor ŋa winlole daal pu ki ŋa mu chain, ba pu? waah nan naan dulnyaa aawan mɔmɔk doo na, liyaadaal ngbaan le u kal ki fuur.

ref: That's how God created the heavens and the earth. The Garden of Eden When the Lord God made the heavens and the earth,
text: Uwumbɔr nan naan dulnyaa aawan mɔmɔk kina la. Edenn Aasaak aabɔr Buyoonn Tidindaan Uwumbɔr nan naan dulnyaa aawan mɔmɔk na,

ref: no grass or plants were growing anywhere. God had not yet sent any rain, and there was no one to work the land.
text: tiwanpunnkaan ni nwanbim aa nan kee punni, ba pu? Uwumbɔr aa nan kee cha utaal nu, uja mu aa nan bi ki ga ko kisaak;

ref: But streams came up from the ground and watered the earth.
text: limaln le nan lir kitiŋ pu le ki soon.

ref: The Lord God took some soil from the ground and made a man. God breathed life into the man, and the man started breathing.
text: Uwumbɔr nan di titan ki di naan uja, le ki fuur libuln ŋa umɔfal ni, le u kpaln unibɔn.

ref: The Lord made a garden in a place called Eden, which was in the east, and he put the man there.
text: Uwumbɔr nan ŋa isui i lu ŋisubil na aasaak, liwipuul wɔb, Edenn aatiŋ ni, le ki di waah naan uja u na ŋa ki ponn ni.

ref: The Lord God filled the garden with all kinds of beautiful trees and fruit trees. Two other trees were in the middle of the garden. One of these gave life—the other gave the wisdom to know the difference between right and wrong.
text: Uwumbɔr nan cha isui i lu ŋisubil nyaan na aabɔŋ punn kisaak ngbaan ni, ŋisubil ngbaan ŋan linimbil chee, ki mɔ lijil. Kisaak ngbaan aakaasisik ni, busub bubaa nan bi, ki ye limɔfal aasub, buken mu nan bi ki ye mbeem aasub, unii yaa jin bu kan, u ga bee ni ŋan na, ni ni kaa ŋan na.

ref: From Eden a river flowed out to water the garden, then it divided into four rivers.
text: Limɔɔl libaa nan bi Edenn aatiŋ ngbaan ni, ki puu lir kisaak ngbaan ni. Limɔɔl ngbaan aah nyan kitiŋ ngbaan ni na, le li yakr ki ŋa ŋimɔɔ ŋinaa.

ref: The first one is the Pishon River that flows through the land of Havilah,
text: Bi yin njan aapaln ke Pisonn, li nan puu dii Hafila aatiŋ ni la.

ref: where pure gold, rare perfumes, and precious stones are found.
text: Salmaa nyaan, ni tulalee, ni ŋitakpanyaan le nan bi nima.

ref: The second is the Gihon River that winds through Ethiopia.
text: Bi yin lelee aapaln ke Gihonn, li nan puu dii Kus aatiŋ ni la.

ref: The Tigris River that flows east of Assyria is the third, and the fourth is the Euphrates River.
text: Bi yin taataa aapaln ke Tigris, li nan puu dii Asiria aawipuul wɔb la; le bi yin linanar ke Yufrates.

ref: The Lord God put the man in the Garden of Eden to take care of it and to look after it.
text: Uwumbɔr nan di uja ngbaan ŋa kisaak ngbaan ni, ke u li ko, ki li lik kisaak ngbaan.

ref: But the Lord told him, “You may eat fruit from any tree in the garden,
text: Uwumbɔr nan bui u ke, “Aa ga ŋmaa ji ŋisubil ŋi mɔmɔk bi kisaak kee ponn ni na,

ref: except the one that has the power to let you know the difference between right and wrong. If you eat any fruit from that tree, you will die before the day is over!”
text: see busub bu aasubil cha unii beer ni ŋan na, ni ni kaa ŋan na. Aa taa ji busub ngbaan aasubil. Aa yaa jin kan, see aa kpo.”

ref: The Lord God said, “It isn't good for the man to live alone. I will make a suitable partner for him.”
text: Tidindaan Uwumbɔr nan ki bui ke, “Naa ŋan ke uja li bi ubaa. M ga naan uŋeer u ga li ter u na ki tii u.”

ref: So the Lord took some soil and made animals and birds. He brought them to the man to see what names he would give each of them. Then the man named the tame animals and the birds and the wild animals. That's how they got their names. None of these was the right kind of partner for the man.
text: Le uja ngbaan nan yin tiwakor, ni ipeel, ni inyoon, ni tiwan nimɔk fu na aayimbil; tɔ, tiwan ngbaan mɔmɔk ponn ni nibaa aa nan ye uŋeer, ki ga ŋmaa ter u.

ref: So the Lord God made him fall into a deep sleep, and he took out one of the man's ribs. Then after closing the man's side,
text: Uwumbɔr nan cha uja ngbaan doon geen, ke linikpol na. Waah geen kina na, le u chuu nyan uja ngbaan aasikaakpab mubaa, ki ki giin ŋeeb.

ref: the Lord made a woman out of the rib. The Lord God brought her to the man,
text: Uwumbɔr nan di nsikaakpab na, ki di naan upii. Uja ngbaan aah finn na, le Uwumbɔr di upii ngbaan dan u chee.

ref: and the man exclaimed, “Here is someone like me! She is part of my body, my own flesh and bones. She came from me, a man. So I will name her Woman!”
text: Le uja ngbaan bui ke, “Aa ye unii le ke maah ye pu na, likpabl li nyan ni maakpab ni na, ni tiwon ti ye maawon na. M ga yin si ke upii, ba pu? aa nyan ni mi uja ni la.”

ref: That's why a man will leave his own father and mother. He marries a woman, and the two of them become like one person.
text: Nima pu le ni ŋan ke uja ni upuu nya cha bitetiib ni binatiib, ki kpaan tɔb chee, ki ŋa unibaan.

ref: Although the man and his wife were both naked, they were not ashamed.
text: Le uja ni upii nan chuun biŋmeen, kaa san inimɔɔn.

ref: The snake was sneakier than any of the other wild animals that the Lord God had made. One day it came to the woman and asked, “Did God tell you not to eat fruit from any tree in the garden?”
text: Uwumbɔr aah nan naan ipeel imɔk na, uwaa le nan kpa nlan ki jer i mɔmɔk. Le u baa upii ngbaan ke, “Uwumbɔr sil bui ke ni taa ji kisaak ngbaan ni aasub bubaa aasubil aa?”

ref: The woman answered, “God said we could eat fruit from any tree in the garden,
text: Le upii ngbaan bui u, “Ti ga ŋmaa ji ŋisubil ngbaan mɔk,

ref: except the one in the middle. He told us not to eat fruit from that tree or even to touch it. If we do, we will die.”
text: see busub bu si kaasisik ni na, le Uwumbɔr bui ke ti taa ji bu aasubil, hali ti yaa meeh ŋi kan, ti ga kpo.”

ref: “No, you won't!” the snake replied.
text: Le uwaa bui ke, “Naa ye mbamɔn, naan kpo.

ref: “God understands what will happen on the day you eat fruit from that tree. You will see what you have done, and you will know the difference between right and wrong, just as God does.”
text: Uwumbɔr nyi ke ni yaa jin ŋi kan, ninimbil le ga woln, le ni ga bee ni ŋan na, ni ni kaa ŋan na, ki ga li bi ke waah bi pu na.”

ref: The woman stared at the fruit. It looked beautiful and tasty. She wanted the wisdom that it would give her, and she ate some of the fruit. Her husband was there with her, so she gave some to him, and he ate it too.
text: Le upii lik ŋisubil ngbaan, ki kan ŋaah ŋan pu na, ki dakl ke unimbil yaa woln kan, ni ga li ŋan sakpen, le ki neen ŋi, le ki ker ŋi ki moo, le ki di ŋiken tii uchal mu, le u mu moo.

ref: At once they saw what they had done, and they realized they were naked. Then they sewed fig leaves together to cover themselves.
text: Baah moo ŋi doo na, le binimbil daa woln, le bi bee ke bi chuun biŋmeen la, nima pu na, le bi bar tifar ki di doŋ binimbiin ni.

ref: Late in the afternoon, when the breeze began to blow, the man and woman heard the Lord God walking in the garden. So they hid behind some trees. Sin Brings a Curse
text: Kijook ngbaan le bi ŋun Tidindaan Uwumbɔr chuun kisaak ni, le bi san ti bɔr isui aakaasisik ni.

ref: The Lord God called out to the man and asked, “Where are you?”
text: Le Tidindaan Uwumbɔr yin uja ngbaan ke, “Aa bi la?”

ref: The man answered, “I was naked, and when I heard you walking through the garden, I was frightened and hid!”
text: Le uja bui u ke, “M ŋun saah chuun kisaak ni pu na; le maah chuun ŋŋmeen na, ijawaan chuu mi, le m bɔr.”

ref: “How did you know you were naked?” God asked. “Did you eat any fruit from that tree in the middle of the garden?”
text: Le Uwumbɔr baa u ke, “Ŋma tuk si ke aa chuun aaŋmeen? Maah bui si ke aa taa ji ŋisubil ŋi na, le aa jin aa?”

ref: “It was the woman you put here with me,” the man said. “She gave me some of the fruit, and I ate it.”
text: Le uja bui u ke, “Saah di upii u tii mi ke u li bi m chee na, uma le tii mi ŋisubil ngbaan, le m moo.”

ref: The Lord God then asked the woman, “What have you done?” “The snake tricked me,” she answered, “and I ate some of that fruit.”
text: Le Uwumbɔr baa upii ngbaan ke, “Ba ŋa aa ŋa kina?” Le upii bui u ke, “Uwaa le ŋmann mi, le m moo.” Uwumbɔr aah daa uwaa, ni upii, ni uja aatafal pu na

ref: So the Lord God said to the snake: “Because of what you have done, you will be the only animal to suffer this curse— For as long as you live, you will crawl on your stomach and eat dirt.
text: Le Uwumbɔr bui uwaa, “Saah ŋa kina na, ipeel mɔmɔk ni, m puun lii sin baanja le pu. Din ki joo cha, aa ga li baar titan ni, ki li ji titan, saamɔfal mɔmɔk ni.

ref: You and this woman will hate each other; your descendants and hers will always be enemies. One of hers will strike you on the head, and you will strike him on the heel.”
text: Aa ni upii ngbaan ga li ye tɔb aadim la. Saabim ni waabim mu ga li ye tɔb aadim la. Uyaabil ga faa wii aayil, le aa mu ga gaa utaagbeln.”

ref: Then the Lord God said to the woman, “You will suffer terribly when you give birth. But you will still desire your husband, and he will rule over you.”
text: Le Uwumbɔr bui upii ke, “Aa ga kan falaa pam timal ponn ni. Ni ki kpee na, aa ga li ban ke aachal li gee si, le u ga li ye saayidaan.”

ref: The Lord said to the man, “You listened to your wife and ate the fruit I told you not to eat. And so, the ground will be under a curse because of what you did. As long as you live, you will have to struggle to grow enough food.
text: Le Uwumbɔr bui uja ngbaan ke, “Saah ŋun tii aapuu ki yii maamɔb ki jin ŋisubil ngbaan na, m puun lii kitiŋ pu. Aa ga tun ki kan falaa le ki nin kan tijikaar ji.

ref: Your food will be plants, but the ground will produce thorns and thistles.
text: Kitiŋ ga pa ikokon ni ichakpeejagar; le aa ga li ji timoor ni aasufar.

ref: You will sweat all your life to earn a living; you were made out of soil, and you will once again turn into soil.”
text: Aa ga ko le kitotoŋ naa si waahr, le aa nin kan tijikaar ki ji, ki ti saa saakundaal. Le aa ga ki gir koo titan ni; ba pu? aa nyan titan ni la. M nan di titan le naan si, le aa ga ki kpaln titan.”

ref: The man Adam named his wife Eve because she would become the mother of all who live.
text: Adam nan yin upii ke Iif; ba pu? uma le ye binib mɔmɔk aana.

ref: Then the Lord God made clothes out of animal skins for the man and his wife.
text: Le Uwumbɔr di tiwakor aagban ki di jab, ki ŋa tiwanpeenkaan ki di peen bi. Uwumbɔr aah nan jenn nyan Adam ni upuu kisaak ngbaan ni pu na

ref: The Lord said, “They now know the difference between right and wrong, just as we do. But they must not be allowed to eat fruit from the tree that lets them live forever.”
text: Tidindaan Uwumbɔr nan bui ke, “Dandana, uja ngbaan ŋa ke timi aah bi pu na, ki bee ni ŋan na, ni ni kaa ŋan na. Taan ki cha u ji limɔfal aasub aasubil mu, aan ki li bi cha n‑yoonn mu kaa kpa ndoon na.”

ref: So the Lord God sent them out of the Garden of Eden, where they would have to work the ground from which the man had been made.
text: Le Uwumbɔr jenn nyan u Edenn aasaak ngbaan ni, le ki cha u ko waah nyan ni titan ti ponn ni na,

ref: Then God put winged creatures at the entrance to the garden and a flaming, flashing sword to guard the way to the life-giving tree.
text: le ki di tiwanfuurkaan ni kpa ifeen na, ki di siin kisaak ngbaan aawipuul wɔb, le ki di mmiigaal mu naahn kijuk ki fenni ŋipepel mɔmɔk na, ki kiir kisaak ngbaan. Nima kan, unii ubaa aan ki pii limɔfal aasub chee.

ref: Adam and Eve had a son. Then Eve said, “I'll name him Cain because I got him with the help of the Lord.”
text: Adam nan doon upuu chee, le u dek lipuul, ki ma ubijabo, le ki bui ke u kan ubo, Uwumbɔr aaterm pu, nima pu na, u ga yin u ke Kainn.

ref: Later she had another son and named him Abel. Abel became a sheep farmer, but Cain farmed the land.
text: Ki joo cha, le Adam aapuu ki dek, le ki ki ma ubijabo, bi yi u ke Abel. Kainn nan ye ukpaal, le Abel mu nan ye upihdaan.

ref: One day, Cain gave part of his harvest to the Lord,
text: Le n‑yoonn mubaa, Kainn nan chee waajikaar, ki joo ni waajikaar ngem, ki nan tii Uwumbɔr ipiin.

ref: and Abel also gave an offering to the Lord. He killed the first-born lamb from one of his sheep and gave the Lord the best parts of it. The Lord was pleased with Abel and his offering,
text: Le Abel mu nan lik waapiih ponn ni waapihninkpel aah pue ma njan aapihbo u na, ki di toor kitork, ki di tinann ti kpa nkpan na ki tii Uwumbɔr. Le Uwumbɔr pak Abel ni waapiin,

ref: but not with Cain and his offering. This made Cain so angry that he could not hide his feelings.
text: le ki yii Kainn ni waapiin. Le Kainn gee liŋuul pam, le unimbil wɔb bii.

ref: The Lord said to Cain: What's wrong with you? Why do you look so angry?
text: Le Uwumbɔr baa u ke, “Ba ŋa aa gee liŋuul? Ba ŋa aanimbil wɔb bii?

ref: If you had done the right thing, you would be smiling. But you did the wrong thing, and now sin is waiting to attack you like a lion. Sin wants to destroy you, but don't let it!
text: Aa yaa kaa ba tun titunwanbir kan, maan pak saapiin aa? Tɔ, saah tun taani na, titunwanbir si gbɔk si, ki ban ti di si ŋa taanaagbiija, see aa nyaŋ ti.”

ref: Cain said to his brother Abel, “Let's go for a walk.” And when they were out in a field, Cain attacked and killed him.
text: Le ni bi cha, le Kainn nan bui unaal Abel ke, [“Cha ti buen timoor ni.”] N‑yoonn mu bi bi timoor ni na, le u ti ku unaal Abel.

ref: Afterwards the Lord asked Cain, “Where is Abel?” “How should I know?” he answered. “Am I supposed to look after my brother?”
text: Nee aapuwɔb le Uwumbɔr baa Kainn ke, “Aanaal Abel bi la?” Le Kainn bui ke, “Maa nyi. Min le ye u lik u na aa?”

ref: Then the Lord said: Why have you done this terrible thing? You killed your own brother, and his blood flowed onto the ground. Now his blood is calling out for me to punish you.
text: Le Uwumbɔr ki baa u ke, “Ba ŋa aa ŋa kina? Aanaal aasin bi kitiŋ ki wii, ki mɔk ke sin le ku u.

ref: And so, I'll put you under a curse. Because you killed Abel and made his blood run out on the ground, you will never be able to farm it again.
text: M ga daa aatafal. Saan ki ŋmaa ko kitiŋ. Saah ku aanaal na, waasin bii kitiŋ tii si a.

ref: When you try to farm the land, it won't produce anything for you. From now on, you'll be without a home, and you'll spend the rest of your life wandering from place to place.
text: Le aa yaa ki ko kan, kitiŋ aan ŋa tijikaar mbamɔm ki tii si, le aa ga li gonni, ni kookoo, ki ga li ye uchayiin dulnyaa wee ni.”

ref: “This punishment is too hard!” Cain said.
text: Le Kainn bui Uwumbɔr ke, “Aa daa ntafal ki ti chaar jer a.

ref: “You're making me leave my home and live far from you. I will have to wander about without a home, and just anyone could kill me.”
text: Aa nyan mi kitiŋ kee pu a, le ki chuu paar aaŋaal m pu. Maan ki li kpa maah ga koo nin chee na. Le maah ga li ye uchayiin pu na, unii umɔk kan mi na ga ku mi.”

ref: “No!” the Lord answered. “Anyone who kills you will be punished seven times worse than I am punishing you.” So the Lord put a mark on Cain to warn everyone not to kill him.
text: Le Uwumbɔr daan Kainn kidaan, ubaa taa ku u, le ki bui ke unii umɔk yaa ku u na kan, bi ga ku binib bilole le ki teen lipɔln.

text: M ga ŋa tinyoor ŋa unii umɔk ŋa tinyoor ŋa aa pu na, ki ga puun lii unii umɔk puun lii aa pu na, le aa pu, m ga ŋa tinyoor ŋa ŋinibol mɔmɔk pu.”

ref: Abram went as far as the sacred tree of Moreh in a place called Shechem. The Canaanites were still living in the land at that time,
text: le Abram chuun dii kitiŋ ngbaan ni, le ki ti fuu bususakpem bu bi yin bu ke More, ki bi Sekem aatiŋ ni na chee. N‑yoonn ngbaan, Kanaann yaab nan laa beenin bi kitiŋ ngbaan ni.

ref: but the Lord appeared to Abram and promised, “I will give this land to your family forever.” Abram then built an altar there for the Lord.
text: Uwumbɔr nan dan Abram chee le ki nan bui u ke, “Kitiŋ kee le m joo m di tii saamaal.” Nima chee le Abram maa kitork aabimbiln tii Uwumbɔr u nan dan u chee na.

ref: The crops failed, and there was no food anywhere in Canaan. So Abram and his wife Sarai went to live in Egypt for a while. But just before they got there, Abram said, “Sarai, you are really beautiful!
text: Baah nan ban bi koo Ijipt aatiŋ ni na, le Abram bui upuu Sarai ke, “Aa ye upii u ŋan na la.

ref: When the Egyptians see you, they will murder me because I am your husband. But they won't kill you.
text: Ijipt yaab yaa kan si kan, bi ga bee ke aa ye mpuu la, le bi ga ku mi, ki gaa si.

ref: Please save my life by saying you are my sister.”
text: Bui bi ke nninkpan le ye si. Nima kan bi ga cha m li bi, ki ŋa mi tiŋann.”

ref: As soon as Abram and Sarai arrived in Egypt, the Egyptians noticed how beautiful she was.
text: Baah ti fuu Ijipt na, Ijipt yaab nan sil kan ke Abram aapuu Sarai ŋan pam.

ref: The king's officials told him about her, and she was taken to his house.
text: Ubɔr ngbaan aaninkpiib nan sil kan Sarai, le ki buen ti bui ubɔr, Sarai aah ŋan sakpen pu na, le ki di u buen ubɔr aachiln ni.

ref: The king was good to Abram because of Sarai, and Abram was given sheep, cattle, donkeys, slaves, and camels.
text: Sarai pu na, ubɔr ngbaan nan ŋa Abram tiŋann pam, le ki tii u ipiih, ni iŋoob, ni inaa, ni ibon, ni binaagbiib, ni laakumii tiib.

ref: Because of Sarai, the Lord struck the king and everyone in his palace with terrible diseases.
text: Tɔ, ubɔr aah nan gaa Sarai, Abram chee na, le Uwumbɔr nan cha iween i bir na lir u ni waachiln aanib pu.

ref: Finally, the king sent for Abram and said to him, “What have you done to me? Why didn't you tell me Sarai was your wife?
text: Iween aah lir ubɔr ni udo yaab pu na, le u tun ki ti yin ni Abram, le ki nan baa u ke, “Ba ŋa aa ŋa mi kina? Ba ŋa saa tuk mi mbamɔn ke u ye aapuu la?

ref: Why did you make me believe she was your sister? Now I've married her. Take her and go! She's your wife.”
text: Ba ŋa aa tuk mi ke u ye aaninkpan la, ki cha m yoor u ki di ŋa mpuu? Aapuu sɔ. Gaa u, ki di u nya kitiŋ kee ni.”

ref: So the king told his men to let Abram and Sarai take their possessions and leave.
text: Ubɔr ngbaan nan cha waanib jenn nyan Abram, ni upuu, ni waawan mɔmɔk, waatiŋ ni.

ref: Abram and Sarai took everything they owned and went to the Southern Desert. Lot went with them.
text: Abram, ni upuu, ni waawan mɔmɔk nan nyan Ijipt aatiŋ ni, ki cha ŋŋangan wɔb, le ki buen Kanaann ŋŋangii wɔb, le Lot mu nan dii u, le bi ti kal nibaa chee.

ref: Abram was very rich. He owned many cattle, sheep, and goats, and had a lot of silver and gold.
text: Abram nan kpa liwangol pam: u nan kpa ipiih, ni iŋoob, ni inaa, ki kpee ŋimombil pam.

ref: Abram moved from place to place in the Southern Desert. And finally, he went north and set up his tents between Bethel and Ai,
text: U nan ki fii nima chee, le ki chuun ki doo nsan ni, ki ti ki fuu Betel ni Ai aakaasisik ni, waah nan ti kal nin chee,

ref: where he had earlier camped and built an altar. There he worshiped the Lord.
text: ki maa kitork aabimbiln na. Le u doon Uwumbɔr nima.

ref: Lot, who was traveling with him, also had sheep, goats, and cattle, as well as his own family and slaves.
text: Lot mu nan kpa ipiih, ni iŋoob, ni inaa, ki kpee waamaal, ni waanaagbiib.

ref: At this time the Canaanites and the Perizzites were still living in the same area, and so there wasn't enough pastureland left for Abram and Lot with all of their animals. Besides this, the men who took care of Abram's animals and the ones who took care of Lot's animals started quarreling.
text: Nima chee, le Abram aanib ni Lot aanib bi nan kpaa baawakor ngbaan na piin ki jaa. N‑yoonn ngbaan, Kanaann yaab ni Peris yaab nan beenin bi kitiŋ ngbaan ni.

ref: Abram said to Lot, “We are close relatives. We shouldn't argue, and our men shouldn't be fighting one another.
text: Abram nan bui Lot ke, “Ti ye mmaal mbaa aanib la, naa ŋan ke timi aanib ngbaan li jaa.

ref: There is plenty of land for you to choose from. Let's separate. If you go north, I'll go south; if you go south, I'll go north.”
text: Cha ti yakr tɔb chee. M tii si nsan, pue lee saah gee lipepel li wɔb na. Nima kan, aa ni saanib ga li bi nibaa wɔb, aan m mu ni maanib li bi niken wɔb.”

ref: This happened before the Lord had destroyed the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah. And when Lot looked around, he saw there was plenty of water in the Jordan Valley. All the way to Zoar the valley was as green as the garden of the Lord or the land of Egypt.
text: Lot nan lik mann gonn, le ki kan ke Jɔɔdann aafataŋ wɔb, ki ti saa Soar aapepel, kpa nnyun sakpen, ke Uwumbɔr aasaak, ni Ijipt aatiŋ na. Nimina mɔmɔk nan laa bi le Uwumbɔr nan nin bii Sodom ni Gomora aatim.

ref: So Lot chose the whole Jordan Valley for himself, and as he started out toward the east, he and Abram separated.
text: Nima pu na, Lot nan lee Jɔɔdann aafataŋ wɔb, le u ni waanib mɔmɔk buen liwipuul aapepel wɔb. Binib bilee ngbaan aah nan yakr tɔb chee pu na le na.

ref: Abram stayed in the land of Canaan. But Lot settled near the cities of the valley and put up his tents not far from Sodom,
text: Abram nan bee Kanaann aatiŋ aapepel, le Lot mu nan kal kifataŋ ni, ntim aakaasisik ni, Sodom chee.

ref: where the people were evil and sinned terribly against the Lord. Abram Moves to Hebron
text: Ntim ngbaan aanib nan ye titunwanbirdam la, le ki tun ni kaa ŋan na Uwumbɔr aanimbiin ni. Abram aah nan buen Hebronn aatiŋ ni pu na

ref: After Abram and Lot had gone their separate ways, the Lord said to Abram: Look around to the north, south, east, and west.
text: Buyoonn Lot nan yakr Abram chee ki buen waah gee ni wɔb na, le Uwumbɔr nan bui Abram ke, “Lik ŋipepel mɔmɔk mbamɔm.

ref: I will give you and your family all the land you can see. It will be theirs forever!
text: M ban ke m di saah waa kitiŋ kimɔk na ki di tii aa ni saamaal, aan ki li ye nimi aatiŋ n‑yoonn mu kaa kpa ndoon na.

ref: I will give you more descendants than there are specks of dust on the earth, and someday it will be easier to count those specks of dust than to count your descendants.
text: M ga cha aa maa ki pɔr pam, le unii ubaa aan ŋmaa kahn saamaal aanib; bi ga li wiir jer dulnyaa wee aatanbol mɔmɔk aah wiir pu na.

ref: Now walk back and forth across the land, because I am going to give it to you.
text: Tɔ, chuun ki mann kitiŋ ngbaan mɔmɔk; ba pu? m ga di kitiŋ ngbaan mɔmɔk ki di tii si.”

ref: Abram took down his tents and went to live near the sacred trees of Mamre at Hebron, where he built an altar in honor of the Lord.
text: Le Abram buen ti kal isui sakpiin i bi yin i ke Mamre aasui na chee, Hebronn aatiŋ ni, le ki maa kitork aabimbiln, le ki doon Uwumbɔr nima chee.

ref: King Chedorlaomer and his allies had ruled these last five kings for twelve years, but in the thirteenth year the kings rebelled and joined forces in Siddim Valley, which is now covered by the southern part of the Dead Sea.
text: Kedolaomer aabɔr le nan joo bi, ki ti saa ŋibin kipiik ni ŋilee, le libiln kipiik ni litatar aayoonn, bibɔrb ngbaan nan kpokl ke baan ki li bi utaab.

ref: A year later King Chedorlaomer and his allies attacked and defeated the Rephaites in Ashteroth-Karnaim, the Zuzites in Ham, and the Emites in Shaveh-Kiriathaim.
text: Ŋibin kipiik ni ŋinaa ni, le ubɔr Kedolaomer, ni bibɔrb biken nan di baajab ti to ki nyaŋ Refa aanib bi nan bi Asterof Kanayim aatiŋ ni na, ni Sus aanib bi nan bi Ham aatiŋ ni na, ni Em aanib bi nan bi Kiriataim aafataŋ ni na,

ref: They also defeated the Horites in the hill country of Edom, as far as El-Paran, near the desert.
text: ni Hor yaab bi nan bi Edom aajoo pu na, ki san dii bi ti saa Elparann aatiŋ ki bi nteersakpiin aagbaan na.

ref: They went back to the city of Enmishpat, better known as Kadesh. Then they captured all the land that belonged to the Amalekites, and they defeated the Amorites who were living in Hazazon-Tamar.
text: Le bi fenn ki mann ki ki gir ni Kades aatiŋ, ki bi nan ki yin ki ke Enn Mispat na. Bi nan to ki nyaŋ Amalek yaab aatim, le ki nyaŋ Amor yaab bi nan bi Hasasonn Tamar ni na.

ref: At Siddim Valley, the armies of the kings of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela fought the armies of King Chedorlaomer of Elam, King Tidal of Goiim, King Amraphel of Babylonia, and King Arioch of Ellasar. The valley
text: le ki ton Elam, ni Goyim, ni Babiloŋ, ni Elasar aabɔrb butɔb. Bibɔrb biŋmu ngbaan le nan fii sil, ki ton bibɔrb binaa ngbaan butɔb.

ref: was full of tar pits, and when the troops from Sodom and Gomorrah started running away, some of them fell into the pits. Others escaped to the hill country.
text: Baah nan bi kifataŋ ki ponn ni na nan kpa kootaal aabuu pam, nima pu na, Sodom ni Gomora aabɔrb ni baajab aah nan ban bi san na, ngem nan lir ŋibuu ngbaan ni; le bi gur na nan san buen ŋijoo ŋibaa paab, le ki ŋmar.

ref: Their enemies took everything of value from Sodom and Gomorrah, including their food supplies.
text: Bibɔrb binaa ngbaan aah nan nyaŋ Sodom ni Gomora aabɔrb na, bi nan yoor tiwan mɔmɔk, ki kpee baajikaar mu, le ki di buen.

ref: They also captured Abram's nephew Lot, who lived in Sodom. They took him and his possessions and then left.
text: Le Lot u nan ye Abram aanaal aajapɔɔn na mu nan bi Sodom aatiŋ ni, le bi nan kpaan chuu u ni waah kpa tiwan nimɔk na, ki di buen.

ref: At this time Abram the Hebrew was living near the oaks that belonged to Mamre the Amorite. Mamre and his brothers Eshcol and Aner were Abram's friends. Someone who had escaped from the battle told Abram
text: Tɔ, unii ubaa le nan san ŋmar, ki buen ti tuk Abram, u ye Hiibru aanii, ki nan ka gbɔk Amor aanii Mamre aasui sakpiin na, le u ni unaatiib Eskol ni Aner nan si Abram aachaŋ ni.

ref: that his nephew Lot had been taken away. Three hundred and eighteen of Abram's servants were fighting men, so he took them and followed the enemy as far north as the city of Dan.
text: N‑yoonn mu Abram nan ŋun ke bi chuu unaal aajapɔɔn Lot na, le u yin waanib bi to butɔb, ki ye binib ikui itaa ni kipiik ni biniin na, le bi mɔmɔk nan san dii bibɔrb binaa ngbaan, ki ti saa Dann aatiŋ ni.

ref: That night, Abram divided up his troops, attacked from all sides, and won a great victory. But some of the enemy escaped to the town of Hobah north of Damascus,
text: Nima chee le Abram nan yakr waanib ngbaan ŋiker ŋiker, le ki buen ti lir waadim ngbaan pu kinyeek, le ki nyaŋ bi. U nan dii bi kina, ki ti saa Hoba aatiŋ ki bi Damaskus aaŋangan wɔb na,

ref: and Abram went after them. He brought back his nephew Lot, together with Lot's possessions and the women and everyone else who had been captured. Abram Is Blessed by Melchizedek
text: le ki gaa baah nan yoor tiwan nimɔk na. U nan gaa ni unaal aajapɔɔn Lot, ni waawan mɔmɔk, ki kpee bipiib, ni binib bi bibɔrb ngbaan nan chuu bi na mɔmɔk. Melkisedek aah nan ŋa tinyoor ŋa Abram pu pu na aabɔr

ref: Abram returned after he had defeated King Chedorlaomer and the other kings. Then the king of Sodom went to meet Abram in Shaveh Valley, which is also known as King's Valley.
text: Buyoonn Abram nan ti nyaŋ ubɔr Kedolaomer, ni bibɔrb biken, aan ki gir choo kun ni na, le Sodom aabɔr nan tooh u nsan pu, le bi nan ti ton tɔb, Safe aafataŋ ki bi yin ki ke Ubɔr Aafataŋ na ni.

ref: King Melchizedek of Salem was a priest of God Most High. He brought out some bread and wine
text: Le Melkisedek u nan ye Salem aabɔr, ki ki ye Uwumbɔr u kaa kpa ŋeen aatɔ na aatotoor na, nan joo ni tijikaar, ni ŋisubil aadaan nan tii Abram,

ref: and said to Abram: “I bless you in the name of God Most High, Creator of heaven and earth.
text: le ki ŋa tinyoor ŋa u pu, ki bui ke, “Uwumbɔr u kaa kpa ŋeen aatɔ, ki naan paacham ni kitiŋ na, ŋa tinyoor ŋa Abram pu.

ref: All praise belongs to God Most High for helping you defeat your enemies.” Then Abram gave Melchizedek a tenth of everything.
text: Uwumbɔr u kaa kpa ŋeen aatɔ, ki cha aa nyaŋ saadim na, kan kipak.” Le Abram nan nyan tiwan nimɔk u nan gaa na nibaabaa kipiik mɔk ponn ni, ki di tii Melkisedek.

ref: The king of Sodom said to Abram, “All I want are my people. You can keep everything else.”
text: Le Sodom aabɔr ngbaan nan bui Abram ke, “Li joo tiwan ngbaan mɔmɔk, ki tee di maanib mɔmɔk tii mi.”

ref: Abram answered: The Lord God Most High made the heavens and the earth. And I have promised him
text: Le Abram bui u ke, “M puu tipuukpaan, Tidindaan Uwumbɔr u kaa kpa ŋeen aatɔ, ki naan paacham ni kitiŋ na aanimbiin ni,

ref: that I won't keep anything of yours, not even a sandal strap or a piece of thread. Then you can never say that you are the one who made me rich.
text: ke maan joo saawan nibaa. Hali ni yaa ye tikokonn, bee linaatakl aagban kan, maa ban ni. Nima kan, saan ŋmaa len ke sin le ŋa Abram uwankpadaan.

ref: Let my share be the food that my men have eaten. But Aner, Eshcol, and Mamre went with me, so give them their share of what we brought back.
text: Maan gaa nibaa ki li joo, see maanib aah jin tijikaar ti nsan ni na. Tɔ, cha Aner, ni Eskol, ni Mamre aanib bi ter mi na kan baah ban pu na.”

ref: Later the Lord spoke to Abram in a vision, “Abram, don't be afraid! I will protect you and reward you greatly.”
text: Nimina aapuwɔb, le Uwumbɔr nan kpiir ubaa mɔk Abram ki bui u ke, “Abram, taa li san ijawaan. M ga kiir tibɔbir mɔmɔk ti ga dan aa pu na, ki tii si ipiin sakpiin.”

ref: But Abram answered, “Lord All-Powerful, you have given me everything I could ask for, except children. And when I die, Eliezer of Damascus will get all I own.
text: Tɔ, Abram nan bui ke, “Ndindaan Uwumbɔr, aa ga tee tii mi ba aapiin? Aa mu kan ke maa kpa ubo. Elieser u ye Damaskus aanii na le ye maafaajir.

ref: You have not given me any children, and this servant of mine will inherit everything.”
text: Saa tii mi ubo, nima pu na, maanaagbiib ponn ni ubaa le ga ji maafaal.”

ref: The Lord replied, “No, he won't! You will have a son of your own, and everything you have will be his.”
text: Le u ki ŋun Uwumbɔr len ke: “Saanaagbiija Elieser aan ji saafaal; see aaponn ni aabo le ga ji saafaal.”

ref: Then the Lord took Abram outside and said, “Look at the sky and see if you can count the stars. That's how many descendants you will have.”
text: Le Uwumbɔr nan cha Abram nyan sil lipaal, le ki bui u ke, “Yaar lik paacham, ki pɔɔk aabaa ki kahn iŋmabi; saamaal ponn ni aanib ga li wiir kina la.”

ref: Abram believed the Lord, so the Lord was pleased with him and accepted him. The Lord Makes Another Promise to Abram
text: Abram nan gaa Uwumbɔr aamɔboln ki kii, kina pu na, Uwumbɔr aasui nan piir Abram pu, le Uwumbɔr gaa u ke u ye uninyaan u chee.

ref: The Lord said to Abram, “I brought you here from Ur in Chaldea, and I gave you this land.”
text: Uwumbɔr nan ki bui u ke, “Min le ye Uwumbɔr u nyan ni si Ur aatiŋ, ki ye Babiloŋ yaab aatiŋ na ni, le ki di kitiŋ kimina tii si, ke ki li ye siyaan.”

ref: Abram asked, “Lord God, how can I know the land will be mine?”
text: Le Abram baa ke, “Ndindaan Uwumbɔr, m ga ŋa kinye ki bee ke kitiŋ ngbaan ga li ye maatiŋ?”

ref: Then the Lord told him, “Bring me a three-year-old cow, a three-year-old female goat, a three-year-old ram, a dove, and a young pigeon.”
text: Le Uwumbɔr nan bui u ke, “Ban ni unaa, ni uŋoob, ni upihja, ni mɔmɔk li ye ŋibin ŋitaa aawakor, ni linanjel, ki kpee libɔrnanjel.”

ref: Abram obeyed the Lord. Then he cut the animals in half and laid the two halves of each animal opposite each other on the ground. But he did not cut the doves and pigeons in half.
text: Le Abram nan joo ni tiwakor ngbaan Uwumbɔr chee, le ki geei ti ŋigen, le ki di ŋigen ngbaan bil toŋ tɔb, ki lur ni bil ikuul ilee; tɔ, waa nan geei inyoon ilee na ma.

ref: And when birds came down to eat the animals, Abram chased them away.
text: Nangbatiib nan dan ke bi nan ji tinann ngbaan, le Abram nan jenn bi.

ref: As the sun was setting, Abram fell into a deep sleep, and everything became dark and frightening.
text: Nwiin aah nan cha mu ti lir na, le ngeen sakpiin nan lir Abram pu, le u doon ki geen, le ijawaan sakpiin chuu u.

ref: Four generations later, your descendants will return here and take this land, because only then will the people who live here be so sinful that they deserve to be punished.
text: Yaabitiib ŋiker ŋinaa pu le ga jer, nee aapuwɔb le saamaal ga ki gir ni kitiŋ kee pu; ba pu? maan jenn nyan Amor yaab kitiŋ kee pu, see baatunwanbir ti nyaŋ, le m nin daa bitafal.”

ref: Sometime after sunset, when it was very dark, a smoking cooking pot and a flaming fire passed between the two halves of each animal.
text: Buyoonn nwiin nan lir, ni bɔnn doo na, le kiŋulk ki kpa iŋmanyuu ni mmiigaal na nan nyan ni, ki jer tiwakor na aanann aakaasisik ni.

ref: At that time the Lord made an agreement with Abram and told him: I will give your descendants the land east of the Shihor River on the border of Egypt as far as the Euphrates River.
text: Nima chee le Uwumbɔr nan puu tipuur tii Abram. U nan bui ke, “M puu ke m ga di kitiŋ kimina tii saamaal, ki yoor Ijipt aatiŋ ki ti saa Yufrates aamɔɔl mɔk,

ref: They will possess the land of the Kenites, the Kenizzites, the Kadmonites,
text: ki kpee Kenn yaab aatim, ni Kenis yaab aatim, ni Kadmonn yaab aatim,

ref: the Hittites, the Perizzites, the Rephaites,
text: ni Hef yaab aatim, ni Peris yaab aatim, ni Refa yaab aatim,

ref: the Amorites, the Canaanites, the Girgashites, and the Jebusites.
text: ni Amor yaab aatim, ni Kanaann yaab aatim, ni Girgas yaab aatim, ni Jebus yaab aatim mɔmɔk.”

ref: Abram's wife Sarai had not been able to have any children. But she owned a young Egyptian slave woman named Hagar,
text: Abram aapuu, u bi nan yin u ke Sarai na, aa nan ma ubo tii u. Tɔ, Ijipt aapiibo ubaa nan bi Sarai chee, ki ye waanaagbiipiibo, bi nan yin u ke Hagar.

ref: and Sarai said to Abram, “The Lord has not given me any children. Sleep with my slave, and if she has a child, it will be mine.” Abram agreed,
text: Sarai nan bui uchal Abram ke, “Uwumbɔr aa tii mi ubo. Saan di maanaagbiipiibo ngbaan, ki di ŋa aapuu, ki kpee m pu uu? Nibaakan, u ga maa ubo tii mi.” Abram nan kii tii upuu Sarai aah len pu na.

ref: and Sarai gave him Hagar to be his wife. This happened after Abram had lived in the land of Canaan for ten years.
text: Nima pu na, Sarai nan di Hagar tii uchal Abram, le u di u ŋa upuu. Nimina nan ŋa buyoonn Abram nan bi Kanaann aatiŋ ni ki ti saa ŋibin kipiik na la.

ref: Later, when Hagar knew she was going to have a baby, she became proud and treated Sarai hatefully.
text: Abram nan doon Hagar chee, le u dek lipuul. Hagar aah kan ke u kpa lipuul na, le waa ki lik pak udindaan Sarai.

ref: Then Sarai said to Abram, “It's all your fault! I gave you my slave woman, but she has been hateful to me ever since she found out she was pregnant. You have done me wrong, and you will have to answer to the Lord for this.”
text: Sarai nan bui uchal Abram ke, “Ni ye saataani pu le Hagar aa ki lik pak mi. Min le nan di u tii si, le waah bee ke u kpa lipuul na, waa ki pak mi. Uwumbɔr le ga lik ki bee m ni si u aabɔr ŋan na.”

ref: Abram said, “All right! She's your slave—do whatever you want with her.” Then Sarai began treating Hagar so harshly that she finally ran away.
text: Le Abram nan bui ke, “Mbamɔn, u ye saanaagbiipiibo la. Sin le joo u. Aa ga ŋmaa ŋa u saah ban pu na.” Le Sarai nan piin ki ŋani Hagar mbiin, le u ti san u chee.

ref: Hagar stopped to rest at a spring in the desert on the road to Shur. While she was there, the angel of the Lord came to her
text: Uwumbɔr aatuun nan ti kan Hagar nnyunbun mubaa chee, mu nan bi kipɔɔk ni, ki dɔ Sur aasan chee,

ref: and asked, “Hagar, where have you come from, and where are you going?” She answered, “I'm running away from Sarai, my owner.”
text: le ki bui u ke, “Hagar, Sarai aanaagbiipii, aa nyan ni la, aan ki cha la?” U nan giin bui u ke, “M san ni ndindaan Sarai chee la.”

ref: The angel said, “Go back to Sarai and be her slave.
text: Le Uwumbɔr aatuun ngbaan nan bui u ke, “Gir buen Sarai chee, ki ti li ye waanaagbiipii.”

ref: I will give you a son, who will be called Ishmael, because I have heard your cry for help. And someday I will give you so many descendants that no one will be able to count them all.
text: Aa ga maa ubijabo, ki yin u ke Ismael; ba pu? Uwumbɔr ŋun saah wii limukl ponn ni pu na.

ref: But your son will live far from his relatives; he will be like a wild donkey, fighting everyone, and everyone fighting him.”
text: Le saabo ngbaan ga li bi ke timoor ni aabon na; u ni biken aan li mɔ tɔb. U ni udo yaab aan ŋmaa kpaan koo tɔb chee.”

ref: Hagar thought, “Have I really seen God and lived to tell about it?” So from then on she called him, “The God Who Sees Me.”
text: Hagar nan baa ubaa ke, “M sil kan Uwumbɔr, le ki beenin bi limɔfal ni, ki ga ŋmaa tuk binib maah kan u pu na aa?” Le u nan yin Uwumbɔr u nan len u chee tibɔr na ke, “Sin le ye Uwumbɔr u waa mi na.”

ref: That's why people call the well between Kadesh and Bered, “The Well of the Living One Who Sees Me.”
text: Nima pu le binib yin nnyunbun mu dɔ Kades ni Bered aakaasisik ni na ke, “U bi ki waa mi na aanyunbun.”

ref: I will keep my solemn promise to you and give you more descendants than can be counted.”
text: M ga puu tipuur tii si ke aa ga li kpa yaabitiib pam.”

ref: Abram bowed with his face to the ground, and God said:
text: Le Abram gbaan Uwumbɔr aanimbiin ni, le unimbil kpaa kitiŋ. Le Uwumbɔr bui u ke,

text: Uwumbɔr nan ŋa tinyoor ŋa Sara pu, waah nan puu ke u ga ŋa pu na,

ref: Although Abraham was very old, Sarah had a son exactly at the time God had said.
text: le u dek lipuul ki ma ubijabo tii uchal Abraham, waah nan pɔr sakpen buyoonn na. U nan ma u buyoonn Uwumbɔr nan len ke bi ga nan maa u na la.

ref: Abraham named his son Isaac,
text: Abraham nan yin u ke Aisak,

ref: and when the boy was eight days old, Abraham circumcised him, just as God had commanded.
text: le ubopɔɔn ngbaan aah nan ti saa iwiin iniin na, le ute Abraham gii uchakpaln, Uwumbɔr aah nan tuk u ke u ŋa pu na.

ref: and Sarah said, “God has made me laugh. And now everyone will laugh with me.
text: Sara nan bui ke, “Uwumbɔr tii mi mpopiin, le ni mu ki kpa tilaar, le unii umɔk ŋun tibɔr tee na ga laa.”

ref: Who would have dared to tell Abraham that someday I would have a child? But in his old age, I have given him a son.”
text: Le u ki len ke, “Ŋma ga ŋmaa bui Abraham ke upuu Sara ga ki maa ubo ki joo u? M ma ubijabo buyoonn u por na.”

ref: The time came when Sarah no longer had to nurse Isaac, and on that day Abraham gave a big feast. Hagar and Ishmael Are Sent Away
text: Aisak nan muun, le waah nan lii libiil na, Abraham nan ŋa njim sakpiin. Abraham aah nan nyan Hagar ni waabo pu na aabɔr

ref: One day, Sarah noticed Hagar's son Ishmael playing, and she said to Abraham, “Get rid of that Egyptian slave woman and her son! I don't want him to inherit anything. It should all go to my son.”
text: Le Sara kan bi, le naa mɔɔr u, le u bui uchal Abraham ke, “Nyan unaagbiipii wee ni waabo aado. Unaagbiipii wee aabo aa kpa nsan u ji lifaal li maabo Aisak ga ji na.”

ref: But God said, “Abraham, don't worry about your slave woman and the boy. Just do what Sarah tells you. Isaac will inherit your family name,
text: Tɔ, Uwumbɔr nan bui Abraham ke, “Taa cha ubo ngbaan ni una aabɔr li muk si sakpen. Ŋa Sara aah len ke aa ŋa pu na. Ni ye mbamɔn ke Aisak pu le m puu ke aa ga kan yaabitiib.

ref: but the son of the slave woman is also your son, and I will make his descendants into a great nation.”
text: M ga cha Ismael mu maa pam, aan bi mu ŋa litimbokpaan. U mu ye aajapɔɔn la.”

ref: Early the next morning Abraham gave Hagar an animal skin full of water and some bread. Then he put the boy on her shoulder and sent them away. They wandered around in the desert near Beersheba,
text: Ni woln kichakpiik ni le Abraham ban tijikaar, le ki ban nnyun ŋa ntuun ni, ki di baln Hagar aabɔgbiln pu, le ki di waabo ngbaan tii u, le ki bui u ke u li cha. Hagar nan chuun le ki yenni Beerseba aateersakpiin ni.

ref: and after they had run out of water, Hagar put her son under a bush.
text: Waah nan joo nnyun mu na nan ti doo, le u di waabo Ismael di bil kitiŋ, busub bubaa taab,

ref: Then she sat down a long way off, because she could not bear to watch him die. And she cried bitterly.
text: le ki buen ti kal nibaa chee ni daa siib ki cha ubo ngbaan. U nan bui ke, “Maan ŋmaa li ka ki kan maabo aah ga kpo pu na.” Le u nan ka nima ki wii.

ref: When God heard the boy crying, the angel of God called out to Hagar from heaven and said, “Hagar, why are you worried? Don't be afraid. I have heard your son crying.
text: Uwumbɔr nan ŋun ubo ngbaan aah wii pu na, le upii ngbaan nan ŋun Uwumbɔr aaneel nyan ni paacham ki baa u ke, “Hagar, ba muk si? Taa san ijawaan, Uwumbɔr ŋun saabo aah wii pu na.

ref: Help him up and hold his hand, because I will make him the father of a great nation.”
text: Fiin, ki buen ti yoor ubo ngbaan ki sɔŋ usui. M ga cha uyaabitiib ŋa litimbokpaan.”

ref: Then God let her see a well. So she went to the well and filled the skin with water, then gave some to her son.
text: Le Uwumbɔr cha unimbil likr, le u kan nnyunbun nima chee. Le u san buen ti lun nnyun nan tii waabo ngbaan, u nyun, le ki lun nnyun gbiin ntuun na mu.

ref: Now I want you to promise in the name of God that you will always be loyal to me and my descendants, just as I have always been loyal to you in this land where you have lived as a foreigner.”
text: Nima pu na, puu Uwumbɔr aanimbiin ni ke saan ŋmann mi, bee maabim, bee n‑yaabitiib. M si aachaŋ ni pam, aa mu puu ke aa ga li si nchaŋ ni, ni kitiŋ kimina aanib pu.”

ref: And so, Abraham promised he would.
text: Abraham nan giin bui u ke, “M puu.”

ref: One day, Abraham told Abimelech, “Some of your servants have taken over one of my wells.”
text: Abraham nan tuk Abimelek waanaagbiib aah jenn uma Abraham aanib ki gaa nnyunbun mubaa pu na.

ref: “This is the first I've heard about it,” Abimelech replied. “Why haven't you said something before? I don't have any idea who did it.”
text: Abimelek nan bui ke, “Maa nyi unii u ŋa kina na. Saa tuk mi tibɔr ngbaan, dandana wee le m laa ŋun ti.”

ref: Abraham gave Abimelech some sheep and cattle, then the two men made a peace treaty.
text: Le Abraham nan tii Abimelek ipiih, ni inaa, le bima bilee ngbaan ŋa kimɔbaan.

ref: Abraham separated seven female lambs from his flock of sheep,
text: Abraham nan yakr ki nyan ipiih i laa diin na ilole, waapiih ponn ni,

ref: and Abimelech asked, “Why have you done this?”
text: le Abimelek baa u ke, “Ba ŋa aa ŋa kina?”

ref: Abraham replied, “I want you to accept these seven lambs as proof that I dug this well.”
text: Abraham nan bui Abimelek ke, “Gaa ipiih ilole yee. Aa yaa gaa ipiih ngbaan kan, ni ga mɔk ke mi Abraham le gbii nnyunbun ngbaan.”

ref: So they called the place Beersheba, because they made a treaty there.
text: Nima pu na, bi nan yin nima chee ke Beerseba, ba pu? nima chee le Abraham ni Abimelek nan puu tii tɔb.

ref: When the treaty was completed, Abimelech and his army commander Phicol went back to the land of the Philistines.
text: Baah nan ti ŋa kimɔbaan Beerseba aanyunbun ngbaan chee doo na, le Abimelek ni Fikol nan gir buen Filistia aatiŋ ni.

ref: Then Abraham lived a long time as a foreigner in the land of the Philistines.
text: Abraham nan bi Filistia aatiŋ ni ki yunn pam.

ref: Some years later God decided to test Abraham, so he spoke to him. Abraham answered, “Here I am, Lord.”
text: N‑yoonn mbaa le Uwumbɔr nan tɔŋ Abraham: u nan yin u ke, “Abraham.” Le Abraham kii ke, “M sɔ.”

ref: The Lord said, “Go get Isaac, your only son, the one you dearly love! Take him to the land of Moriah, and I will show you a mountain where you must sacrifice him to me on the fires of an altar.”
text: Uwumbɔr nan bui u ke, “Di aajapɔɔn, u ye aajapɔnbaal Aisak, aan aa gee u sakpen na, ki di u buen Moria aatiŋ ni. Nima chee m ga mɔk si lijool libaa paab, aa di u toor kitork tii mi nima chee.”

ref: So Abraham got up early the next morning and chopped wood for the fire. He put a saddle on his donkey and set out with Isaac and two servants for the place where God had told him to go.
text: Naah woln kichakpiik ni na, le Abraham ban idɔ ki di paan waabon pu, le ki yoor Aisak ni waanaagbiib bilee ki di kpee ubaa pu. Le bi piin ki cha Uwumbɔr aah nan mɔk u kitiŋ ki na ni.

ref: Three days later Abraham looked off in the distance and saw the place.
text: Iwiin itaa daal, le bi nan lik ki waa kitiŋ ngbaan dandar.

ref: He told his servants, “Stay here with the donkey, while my son and I go over there to worship. We will come back.”
text: Le Abraham nan bui waanaagbiib na ke, “Li si gbɔk ubon do chee na. M ni njapɔɔn ga buen ti doon Uwumbɔr, aan ki gir ni, ni chee.”

ref: Abraham put the wood on Isaac's shoulder, but he carried the hot coals and the knife. As the two of them walked along,
text: Abraham nan cha Aisak yoor idɔ i u ga ti di see kitork ngbaan na, le uma tee joo kijuk ni mmii.

ref: Isaac said, “Father, we have the coals and the wood, but where is the lamb for the sacrifice?” “My son,” Abraham answered, “God will provide the lamb.” The two of them walked on, and
text: Abraham nan giin bui u ke, “Uwumbɔr le ga tii timi kitork aapihbo.” Le bima bilee ngbaan nan chuun cha.

ref: when they reached the place that God had told him about, Abraham built an altar and placed the wood on it. Next, he tied up his son and put him on the wood.
text: Baah nan ti fuu nin chee Uwumbɔr nan mɔk Abraham na, le u maa kitork aabimbiln, le ki koo idɔ paan li pu, le ki chuu ujapɔɔn ki buu u iŋmin, ki di paan idɔ pu, libimbiln na paab,

ref: He then took the knife and got ready to kill his son.
text: le ki yoor kijuk ke u kɔr ujapɔɔn ngbaan.

ref: But the Lord's angel shouted from heaven, “Abraham! Abraham!” “Here I am!” he answered.
text: Le Uwumbɔr aatuun aaneel nyan ni paacham ki yin ke, “Abraham, Abraham.” Le u giin kii ke, “M sɔ.”

ref: “Don't hurt the boy or harm him in any way!” the angel said. “Now I know that you truly obey God, because you were willing to offer him your only son.”
text: Le nneel ngbaan bui ke, “Taa ku ubo ngbaan, ki taa ŋa u nibaa. Dandana wee, m bee ke aa san Uwumbɔr ijawaan, ba pu? saa yii ke saan di aajapɔnbaal na ki di toor kitork tii Uwumbɔr.”

ref: Abraham looked up and saw a ram caught by its horns in the bushes. So he took the ram and sacrificed it instead of his son.
text: Abraham nan yaar ki lik, le ki kan upihja si timoor ni, uyiin lɔk busub waatiir bubaa ponn ni. Le u buen ti chuu upihja ngbaan ki nan di toor kitork tii Uwumbɔr, kaa ki di ujapɔɔn toor.

ref: Abraham named that place “The Lord Will Provide.” And even now people say, “On the mountain of the Lord it will be provided.”
text: Abraham nan yin nima chee ke, “Uwumbɔr le tii.” Ki nan saa din binib beenin len ke, “Uwumbɔr aajool paab le u tii.”

ref: The Lord's angel called out from heaven a second time:
text: Uwumbɔr aatuun aaneel nan ki nyan ni paacham lelee, ki bui Abraham ke,

ref: You were willing to offer your only son to the Lord, and so he makes you this solemn promise,
text: “M ga puu maayimbil pu ke m ga ŋa tinyoor ŋa aa pu pam. Min Uwumbɔr le len kina. Saah ŋa pu, kaa yii ke saan di aajapɔɔn tii mi na,

ref: “I will bless you and give you such a large family, that someday your descendants will be more numerous than the stars in the sky or the grains of sand along the seashore. They will defeat their enemies and take over the cities where their enemies live.
text: nima pu le m puu ke m ga cha aayaabitiib li wiir ke iŋmabi aah bi paacham pu na, ki ga ki li wiir ke limɔɔtanbol li dɔ nnyusakpem aagbaan na. Aayaabitiib le ga li nyaŋni baadim.

ref: You have obeyed me, and so you and your descendants will be a blessing to all nations on earth.”
text: Aayaabitiib pu, le m ga ŋa tinyoor ŋa ŋinibol mɔmɔk pu, ni mu ye le ke aa kii maamɔb la.”

ref: Abraham and Isaac went back to the servants who had come with him, and they returned to Abraham's home in Beersheba. The Children of Nahor
text: Abraham ni ujapɔɔn nan ki gir buen waanaagbiib bilee, ni ubon na chee, le bi dii buen Beerseba aatiŋ ni, nin chee Abraham nan ti kal na. Nahor aayaabitiib aabɔr

ref: Nahor also had another wife. Her name was Reumah, and she had four sons: Tebah, Gaham, Tahash, and Maacah.
text: Reuma, u nan ye Nahor aapuu uken na mu le nan ma Teba, ni Gaham, ni Tahas, ni Maaka.

ref: “I live as a foreigner in your land, and I don't own any property where I can bury my wife. Please let me buy a piece of land.”
text: “M ye uwɔwɔnja, le ki nan bi ni chee; kooh nimi aatiŋ ngbaan nibaa chee tii mi aan m di mpuu sub nima.”

ref: “Sir,” they answered, “you are an important man. Choose the best place to bury your wife. None of us would refuse you a resting place for your dead.”
text: “Uninkpel, li pel taah len pu na, ti lik si ke aa ye uninyuun la; di aapuu ti sub likaakul li ŋan aa gee li na ponn ni. Ti mɔmɔk ga li gee ke ti tii si likaakul li aa ban na, aan aa di aapuu sub li ni.”

ref: Abraham bowed down
text: Le Abraham nan gbaan binimbiin ni,

ref: and replied, “If you are willing to let me bury my wife here, please ask Zohar's son Ephron
text: ki bui bi ke, “Njɔtiib, ni yaa gee ke m di mpuu sub nimi aatiŋ pu kan, mee Sohar aajapɔɔn Efronn,

ref: Ephron was sitting there near the city gate, when Abraham made this request, and he answered,
text: Efronn mu nan ka gbɔk Hef yaab biken aah kuuni kaa nin chee kitiŋ ngbaan aabisamɔb chee na; Efronn nan bui Abraham, binib ngbaan mɔmɔk aanimbil ni ke,

ref: “Sir, the whole field, including the cave, is yours. With my own people as witnesses, I freely give it to you as a burial place for your dead.”
text: “Li pel, uninkpel, m ga di bukpaab ngbaan mɔmɔk, ni kitakpaluŋ ki bi bu ponn ni na mu ki di tii si. M ga di ki tii si, ndo yaab aanimbil ni, nima kan, aa ga ŋmaa di aapuu sub.”

ref: Once again, Abraham bowed down
text: Tɔ, Abraham nan gbaan Hef yaab ngbaan aanimbiin ni,

ref: and said to Ephron, “In front of these witnesses, I offer you the full price, so I can bury my wife. Please accept my offer.”
text: le ki bui Efronn, binib ngbaan mɔmɔk aatafal ni ke, “M gaŋ si, li pel, m ga li gee ke m daa kitiŋ ngbaan mɔmɔk. M ga pa saah ban pu na, aan ki di mpuu sub nima chee.”

ref: So Abraham buried his wife Sarah in Machpelah Cave that was in the field
text: Le Abraham nan di upuu Sara sub kitakpaluŋ ngbaan ponn ni, Kanaann aatiŋ ni.

ref: Abraham was now a very old man. The Lord had made him rich, and he was successful in everything he did.
text: N‑yoonn mue, Abraham nan por pam, le Uwumbɔr nan ŋa tinyoor ŋa waatuln mɔmɔk pu.

ref: One day, Abraham called in his most trusted servant and said to him, “Solemnly promise me
text: U nan bui waanaagbiijapol u lik waatuln mɔmɔk na, ke u di uŋaal ŋa uma Abraham aataapaln taab, ki puu tipuur ke,

ref: in the name of the Lord, who rules heaven and earth, that you won't choose a wife for my son Isaac from the people here in the land of Canaan.
text: “M ban ke aa puu Uwumbɔr u ye paacham ni taab aadindaan na aayimbil pu, ke saan ban Kanaann aatiŋ kee pu aapiibo tii njapɔɔn.

ref: Instead, go back to the land where I was born and find a wife for him from among my relatives.”
text: See aa buen baah nan ma mi kitiŋ ki ni na, ndo yaab ni, ki ti ban upii tii njapɔɔn Aisak.”

ref: But the servant asked, “What if the young woman I choose refuses to leave home and come here with me? Should I send Isaac there to look for a wife?”
text: Tɔ, unaagbiija ngbaan nan baa u ke, “Nibaakan, aado aapiibo ngbaan yaa ti bui ke waan nya udo aatiŋ ni ki dan Kanaann aatiŋ ni kan, m giin aajapɔɔn buen saah nyan ni nin chee na aa?”

ref: “No!” Abraham answered. “Don't ever do that, no matter what.
text: Abraham nan bui u ke, “Lik suuna, ki taa di njapɔɔn gir buen ndo aatiŋ ni.

ref: The Lord who rules heaven brought me here from the land where I was born and promised that he would give this land to my descendants forever. When you go back there, the Lord will send his angel ahead of you to help you find a wife for my son.
text: Uwumbɔr u ye paacham aadindaan na, uma le nyan ni mi nte do, ni ndo yaab aatiŋ ni, le ki puu ke u ga di kitiŋ kimina tii m ni n‑yaabitiib. Uma le ga cha waatuun loln aa pu nsan, nima kan, aa ga ti kan upii ki nan tii njapɔɔn.

ref: If the woman refuses to come along, you don't have to keep this promise. But don't ever take my son back there.”
text: Ni yaa ye ke upiibo ngbaan yii ke waan dii ni si kan, naa ki ye saataani, aa nyan tipuur ngbaan ni a. Tɔ, ni yaa ye pu na kan, aa taa di njapɔɔn buen ndo aatiŋ ni.”

ref: So the servant gave Abraham his word that he would do everything he had been told to do.
text: Nima le unaagbiija ngbaan nan di uŋaal paan udindaan Abraham aataapan pu, le ki puu tipuur ke u ga ŋa waah len pu na mɔmɔk.

ref: Soon after that, the servant loaded ten of Abraham's camels with valuable gifts. Then he set out for the city in northern Syria, where Abraham's brother Nahor lived.
text: Unaagbiijapol u nan joo Abraham aawan mɔmɔk na nan nyan waaninkpel Abraham aalaakumii kipiik, ni tiwan pam, le ki buen Abraham aanaal Nahor aah bi kitiŋ ki na ni, ki bi Mesopotamia aatiŋ aaŋangan wɔb la.

ref: When he got there, he let the camels rest near the well outside the city. It was late afternoon, the time when the women came out for water.
text: Waah ti fuu nima na, le u nan cha laakumii tiib ngbaan doon kitiŋ, nnyunbun mubaa chee, kitiŋ ngbaan aagbaan. Ni nan ye kijook wɔb, buyoonn bipiib chaa ikpen bi ti lu nnyun na.

ref: The servant prayed: You, Lord, are the God my master Abraham worships. Please keep your promise to him and let me find a wife for Isaac today.
text: Unaagbiija ngbaan nan bi mee Uwumbɔr ke, “Uwumbɔr, u ye maaninkpel Abraham Aawumbɔr na, cha m kan maah ban pu na, ki gbiin saah puu ke aa ga ŋa pu ki tii maaninkpel na.

ref: The young women of the city will soon come to this well for water,
text: M bi nnyunbun mu chee kitiŋ kee aasapɔm ga dan, ke bi nan lu nnyun na.

ref: and I'll ask one of them for a drink. If she gives me a drink and then offers to get some water for my camels, I'll know she is the one you have chosen and that you have kept your promise to my master.
text: M ga bui bi ponn ni ubaa ke, ‘Mbisal, tii mi nnyun m nyu.’ U yaa bui mi ke, ‘Nyum, le m ga ki lu nnyun tii laakumii tiib bee mu aan bi nyu’ kan, nima le ga cha m bee ke uma le aa nyan ke u li ye Aisak aapuu. Ni yaa ŋa kina kan, m ga bee ke aa sil gbiin tipuur ti aa nan puu tii maaninkpel na.”

ref: Abraham's servant ran to her and said, “Please let me have a drink of water.”
text: Le unaagbiija ngbaan san buen u chee ki ti bui ke, “Mbisal, tii mi nnyun m nyu.”

ref: “I'll be glad to,” she answered. Then she quickly took the jar from her shoulder and held it while he drank.
text: U nan bui unaagbiija ngbaan ke, “Nyum, uninkpel,” le ki ŋa mala le ki bar nnyun tii u, le u nyun.

ref: After he had finished, she said, “Now I'll give your camels all the water they want.” She quickly poured out water for them, and she kept going back for more, until his camels had drunk all they wanted.
text: U yaa lun ni bi nan nyun doo kan, le u ki san mala buen ki ti ki lu ni nan tii bi, le bi mɔmɔk ti nyun bab.

ref: Abraham's servant did not say a word, but he watched everything Rebekah did, because he wanted to know for certain if this was the woman the Lord had chosen.
text: Unaagbiija na nan si lik upiibo ngbaan suuna, u nan ban u bee ke ni ye Uwumbɔr aageehn aan naa ye.

ref: The servant had brought along an expensive gold ring and two large gold bracelets. When Rebekah had finished bringing the water, he gave her the ring for her nose and the bracelets for her arms.
text: Waah nan lun nnyun tii laakumii tiib ngbaan mɔmɔk doo na, le uja ngbaan nyan salmaa aakur aabɔmbik ki kpa kidaak na, ni salmaa aakur aabuen ilee, ki di tii upiibo ngbaan,

ref: Then he said, “Please tell me who your father is. Does he have room in his house for me and my men to spend the night?”
text: le ki nan baa u ke, “Mbisal, aate ye ŋma? Mpaan ga li bi aate do aan m ni maanib bee ga doon kinyeek kimina aa?”

ref: She answered, “My father is Bethuel, the son of Nahor and Milcah.
text: Upiibo ngbaan nan bui ke, “Nte le ye Betuel, u ye Nahor ni upuu Milka aajapɔɔn na.

ref: We have a place where you and your men can stay, and we also have enough straw and feed for your camels.”
text: Laakumii aajikaar bi nte do, le mpaan mu bi, ni ga kan ki doon.”

ref: Then the servant bowed his head and prayed,
text: Le uja ngbaan gbaan kitiŋ ki doon Uwumbɔr.

ref: “I thank you, Lord God of my master Abraham! You have led me to his relatives and kept your promise to him.”
text: U nan bui ke, “M pak Uwumbɔr u ye maaninkpel Abraham Aawumbɔr, ki gbiin waah nan puu tipuur ti tii u na. U cha m nan tuur maaninkpel Abraham aamaal aachiln ni aanii le pu.”

ref: Rebekah ran straight home and told her family everything.
text: Le upiibo na san buen ti tuk una waah kan pu na mɔmɔk.

ref: Her brother Laban heard her tell what the servant had said, and he saw the ring and the bracelets she was wearing. So Laban ran out to Abraham's servant, who was standing by his camels at the well.
text: Labann nan kan kibɔmbik ni ibuen i nan bi uninkpan aaŋaal pu na, ki ŋun uja ngbaan aah tuk uninkpan pu na. Labann nan buen Abraham aanaagbiija ni waalaakumii tiib aah si nnyunbun mu taab na chee,

ref: Then Laban said, “The Lord has brought you safely here. Come home with me. There's no need for you to keep on standing outside. I have a room ready for you in our house, and there's also a place for your camels.”
text: le ki ti bui u ke, “Dan ti buen ndo, aa ye unii u Uwumbɔr ŋa tinyoor ŋa aa pu na la. Ba ŋa aa nan si do kina? Mpaan bi ndo aa ga ti kan ki doon, le mpaan bi, saalaakumii tiib mu ga koo.”

ref: Abraham's servant went home with Laban, where Laban's servants unloaded his camels and gave them straw and feed. Then they brought water into the house, so Abraham's servant and his men could wash their feet.
text: Nima pu na, uja ngbaan nan dii u buen udo, le Labann nan nyan ŋitun mɔmɔk waalaakumii tiib pu, le ki tii bi timoor ke bi ŋmɔ, le ki ban nnyun tii uja ngbaan ni waanib bi finn bitaa.

ref: After that, they brought in food. But the servant said, “Before I eat, I must tell you why I have come.” “Go ahead and tell us,” Laban answered.
text: Baah nan di tijikaar siin u na, uja ngbaan nan bui ke, “Maan ji tijikaar see m bui nimi maah joo tibɔr ti na mɔmɔk.” Labann nan bui u ke, “Len saah joo pu na.”

ref: The servant explained: I am Abraham's servant.
text: Le u bui Labann ke, “M ye Abraham aanaagbiija la.

ref: The Lord has been good to my master and has made him very rich. He has given him many sheep, goats, cattle, camels, and donkeys, as well as a lot of silver and gold, and many slaves.
text: Uwumbɔr ŋa tinyoor sakpen ki ŋa u pu, le u ŋa uwankpadaan. Uwumbɔr tii u ipiih, ni iŋoob, ni inaa, ni tikunabr, ni salmaa aamombil, ni binaagbiib, ni laakumii tiib, ni ibon.

ref: Sarah, my master's wife, didn't have any children until she was very old. Then she had a son, and my master has given him everything.
text: Le maaninkpel aapuu Sara por pam, tɔ, Uwumbɔr tii u ubijabo, le maaninkpel di waawangol mɔmɔk di tii waabo ngbaan.

ref: Instead, go back to the land where I was born and find a wife for my son from among my relatives.”
text: ke m dan ute do, waamaal aanib chee, ki nan ban upii tii waabo ngbaan.

ref: I asked my master, “What if the young woman refuses to come with me?”
text: Le m baa u ke, ‘Nibaakan, upiibo ngbaan yaa ti yii ke waan buen Kanaann aatiŋ ni kan, m ga ŋa kinye?’

ref: My master answered, “I have always obeyed the Lord, and he will send his angel to help you find my son a wife from among my own relatives.
text: Le u bui mi ke Uwumbɔr, u uma Abraham kii waamɔb na le ga cha waatuun dii mi buen ki ter mi, ke m ga kan upii u nyan ni waamaal ni na, ki nan tii ujapɔɔn.

ref: But if they refuse to let her come back with you, then you are freed from your promise.”
text: U nan ki bui ke maah ga ŋa pu ki nya tipuur ngbaan ni na le ye ke m yaa buen udo yaab chee aan bi yaa yii mi kan, maa ki kpa taani ubaa.

ref: When I came to the well today, I silently prayed, “You, Lord, are the God my master Abraham worships, so please lead me to a wife for his son
text: “N‑yoonn mu m fuu ni nnyunbun chee din wee na, le m mee Uwumbɔr u ye maaninkpel Abraham Aawumbɔr na ke u ter mi,

ref: while I am here at the well. When a young woman comes out to get water, I'll ask her to give me a drink.
text: ki len ke, ‘Maah si gbɔk nnyunbun mu na, usapɔɔn ubaa yaa fuu ni ke u lu nnyun kan, m ga bui u ke u tii mi nnyun m nyu.

ref: If she gives me a drink and offers to get some water for my camels, I'll know she is the one you have chosen.”
text: U yaa kii, ki mu yaa bui ke u ga tii maalaakumii tiib mu nnyun kan, nima le ga cha m bee ke uma le aa nyan tii maaninkpel aajapɔɔn.’

ref: Even before I had finished praying, Rebekah came by with a water jar on her shoulder. When she had filled the jar, I asked her for a drink.
text: Waahr aan m mee Uwumbɔr nsui ni ti doo na, le Rebeka fuu ni nnyunbun ngbaan chee ki joo libuul u nan lu nnyun. Le m bui u ke, ‘Mbisal, tii mi nnyun m nyu.’

ref: She quickly lowered the jar from her shoulder and said, “Have a drink. Then I'll get water for your camels.” So I drank, and after that she got some water for my camels.
text: U ŋa mala le ki bar nnyun tii mi le ki bui ke, ‘Nyum, le m ga lu nnyun tii saalaakumii tiib mu.’ Le m nyun, le u lun nnyun tii laakumii tiib mu.

ref: I asked her who her father was, and she answered, “My father is Bethuel the son of Nahor and Milcah.” At once I put the ring in her nose and the bracelets on her arms.
text: Le m baa u ke, ‘Aate ye ŋma?’ Le u bui ke, ‘Nte le ye Betuel, u ye Nahor ni Milka aajapɔɔn na.’ Nima pu le m di kibɔmbik ni ibuen di ŋmaan uŋaal,

ref: Then I bowed my head and gave thanks to the God my master Abraham worships. The Lord had led me straight to my master's relatives, and I had found a wife for his son.
text: le ki gbaan kitiŋ nima chee, ki pak Uwumbɔr u ye maaninkpel Abraham Aawumbɔr na, ba pu? uma le ter mi, le m di nan tuur waamaal aanib do, le ki kan bibisal tii ujapɔɔn.

ref: Now please tell me if you are willing to do the right thing for my master. Will you treat him fairly, or do I have to look for another young woman?
text: Tɔ, ni yaa kii ke ni ga ŋa maaninkpel aah ban pu na kan, tuk mi; ni yaa kaa ti ki kii kan, tuk mi, aan m mu dakl lik maah ga ŋa pu na.”

ref: Laban and Bethuel answered, “The Lord has done this. We have no choice in the matter.
text: Le Labann ni Betuel bui ke, “Tibɔr ngbaan nyan ni Uwumbɔr chee la. Taan ŋmaa len tibaa.

ref: Take Rebekah with you; she can marry your master's son, just as the Lord has said.”
text: Uwumbɔr le yaa ban kina kan, Rebeka sɔ, li joo u cha, ki ti di u tii saaninkpel aajapɔɔn.”

ref: Abraham's servant bowed down and thanked the Lord.
text: Abraham aanaagbiija ngbaan aah nan ŋun kina na, le u gbaan kitiŋ ki doon Uwumbɔr.

ref: Then he gave clothing, as well as silver and gold jewelry, to Rebekah. He also gave expensive gifts to her brother and her mother.
text: Le u nyan ŋikeken, ni tikunabr, ni salmaa aatakpanyaan tii Rebeka. U nan ki tii upiibo ngbaan aana ni uninja tiwan ni kpa kidaak na.

ref: Abraham's servant and the men with him ate and drank, then spent the night there. The next morning they got up, and the servant told Rebekah's mother and brother, “I would like to go back to my master now.”
text: N‑yoonn ngbaan le unaagbiija na, ni waanib nin jin tijikaar ki nyun mu, le ki doon woln kitaak. Naah woln na, le bi fii, le u bui ke, “M ga gir kun maaninkpel chee la.”

ref: “Let Rebekah stay with us for a week or ten days,” they answered. “Then she may go.”
text: Le Rebeka aaninja ni una nan bui u ke, “Cha upiibo ngbaan ŋa likpaakool, bee iwiin kipiik ti chee, aan aa li joo u chaa kun.”

ref: But he said, “Don't make me stay any longer. The Lord has already helped me find a wife for my master's son. Now let us return.”
text: U mu nan giin bui bi ke, “Taa cha ti gaar do. Uwumbɔr ter mi pam, cha m gir kun maaninkpel chee.”

ref: They answered, “Let's ask Rebekah what she wants to do.”
text: Le Rebeka aana ni uninja bui ke, “Cha ti yin Rebeka ki nan baa u lik.”

ref: They called her and asked, “Are you willing to leave with this man at once?” “Yes,” she answered.
text: Nima pu na, bi nan yin u le ki nan baa u ke, “Aa ga dii uja ngbaan buen aa?” Le u nan kii ke u ga dii u buen la.

ref: So they agreed to let Rebekah and an old family servant woman leave immediately with Abraham's servant and his men.
text: Nima na, bi nan cha Rebeka ni waanaagbiipii u nan lik u tibir ni na dii Abraham aanaagbiija na, ni waanib buen.

ref: They gave Rebekah their blessing and said, “We pray that God will give you many children and grandchildren and that he will help them defeat their enemies.”
text: Baah nan len tinyoor aaliin i ki ŋa Rebeka pu na sɔ: “Tininkpan, Uwumbɔr pee aa pu mmɔɔnyunsɔm aan aa maa pam; ki cha aayaabitiib nyaŋ baadim mɔmɔk.”

ref: Afterwards, Rebekah and the young women who were to travel with her prepared to leave. Then they got on camels and left with Abraham's servant and his men.
text: N‑yoonn ngbaan le Rebeka ni waanaagbiisapɔmbim mɔmɔk gor, le ki diŋ laakumii tiib pu, ki dii Abraham aanaagbiija ngbaan buen.

ref: At that time Isaac was living in the southern part of Canaan near a place called “The Well of the Living One Who Sees Me.”
text: Tɔ, Aisak nan buen kipɔɔk ni, nin chee bi yin ke, “U bi ki waa mi na aanyunbun na” chee, ki nan bi Kanaann aatiŋ ngbaan aaŋangii wɔb la.

ref: One evening he was walking out in the fields, when suddenly he saw a group of people approaching on camels. So he started toward them. Rebekah saw him coming; she got down from her camel, and asked, “Who is that man?” “He is my master Isaac,” the servant answered. Then Rebekah covered her face with her veil.
text: le ki baa Abraham aanaagbiija na ke, “Ŋma ye unachipɔɔn u chuun ikpaakon ni ki choo na?” U nan bui ke, “Maaninkpel ye.” Nima pu na, Rebeka nan di kikekengeŋ biin unimbil wɔb.

ref: The servant told Isaac everything that had happened.
text: Unaagbiija ngbaan nan tuk Aisak waah ŋa pu na mɔmɔk.

ref: Isaac took Rebekah into the tent where his mother had lived before she died, and Rebekah became his wife. He loved her and was comforted over the loss of his mother.
text: Le Aisak di Rebeka di koon una Sara aadiik ni, le ki di u ŋa upuu. Aisak nan gee Rebeka pam, le usui nan sɔŋ ki cha u suln una aakun bɔr.

ref: and they had six sons: Zimran, Jokshan, Medan, Midian, Ishbak, and Shuah.
text: Ketura mu nan ma Simrann, ni Joksann, ni Medann, ni Midiann, ni Isbak, ni Sua, ki tii Abraham.

ref: Later, Jokshan became the father of Sheba and Dedan, and when Dedan grew up, he had three sons: Asshurim, Letushim, and Leummim.
text: Joksann le nan ye Seba ni Dedann aate, le Dedann aayaabitiib nan ye Asur tiib, ni Letus tiib, ni Leum tiib.

ref: Midian also had five sons: Ephah, Epher, Hanoch, Abida, and Eldaah.
text: Midiann aajapɔtiib le nan ye Efa, ni Efer, ni Hanok, ni Abida, ni Eldaa. Bimina mɔmɔk nan ye Ketura aayaabitiib la.

ref: While Abraham was still alive, he gave gifts to the sons of Hagar and Keturah. He also sent their sons to live in the east far from his son Isaac, and when Abraham died, he left everything to Isaac. The Death of Abraham
text: Waah nan laa beenin fu na, u nan ki di ngem mu tii upuutiib biken aah nan ma mbim mu tii u na mu. U nan yakr ujapɔtiib ngbaan di buen liwipuul wɔb aatiŋ kibaa ni, ki cha ujapɔɔn Aisak. Abraham aah nan kpo, ni baah sub u pu na aabɔr

ref: His sons Isaac and Ishmael buried him east of Hebron in Machpelah Cave that was part of the field Abraham had bought from Ephron son of Zohar the Hittite. Abraham was buried there beside his wife Sarah.
text: Bukpaab ngbaan le Abraham nan daa Hef yaab chee na, le bi nan di Abraham sub upuu Sara chee.

ref: God blessed Isaac after this, and Isaac moved to a place called “The Well of the Living One Who Sees Me.” Ishmael's Descendants
text: Abraham aah nan kpo na, le Uwumbɔr nan ŋa tinyoor ŋa Abraham aabo Aisak pu, le u nan bi nnyunbun mu bi nan yin mu ke “U bi ki waa mi na aanyunbun” chee. Ismael aamaal aabɔr (

ref: Ishmael was the son of Abraham and Hagar, the slave woman of Sarah.
text: Abraham le nan ma Ismael mu, una le nan ye Hagar, u nan ye Ijipt yaab aapiibo la, le ki nan ye Sara aanaagbiipii.

ref: Ishmael had twelve sons, in this order: Nebaioth, Kedar, Adbeel, Mibsam,
