ref: Mishma, Dumah, Massa,
text: ni Misma, ni Duma, ni Masa,

ref: Hadad, Tema, Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah.
text: ni Hadad, ni Tema, ni Jetur, ni Nafis, ni Kedema.

ref: Each of Ishmael's sons was a tribal chief, and a village was named after each of them.
text: Bijab kipiik ni bilee ngbaan le aanib nan ŋa ŋitimbol kipiik ni ŋilee, ki di biyaajatiib ngbaan aayimbil yin baatim.

ref: Ishmael had settled in the land east of his brothers, and his sons settled everywhere from Havilah to Shur, east of Egypt on the way to Asshur. Ishmael was
text: Ismael aayaabitiib nan bi Hafila ni Sur aakaasisik ni, ki cha Ijipt aawipuul wɔb ki bi Asiria aasan chee na. Bi ni Abraham aamaal biken aa nan kpaan bi tɔb chee. Aisak aah nan ma Esɔ ni Jakob pu na aabɔr

ref: Isaac was the son of Abraham,
text: Abraham aajapɔɔn Aisak aabɔr sɔ:

ref: Rebekah still had no children. So Isaac asked the Lord to let her have a child, and the Lord answered his prayer.
text: Rebeka aah nan bi ki yunn kaa ma pu na, le uchal Aisak nan mee Uwumbɔr tii u. Le Uwumbɔr nan ŋun waameel ngbaan ki cha Rebeka dek lipuul.

ref: Before Rebekah gave birth, she knew she was going to have twins, because she could feel them inside her, fighting each other. She thought, “Why is this happening to me?” Finally, she asked the Lord why her twins were fighting,
text: U nan dek bijaam aapuul la, le bi nan bi uponn ni ki leer tɔb. Rebeka nan bui ke, “Ba ŋa mbimbin kina aaboln dan m pu?” Le u nan mee Uwumbɔr ke u cha u bee mbimbin ngbaan aatataa.

ref: and he told her: “Your two sons will become two separate nations. The younger of the two will be stronger, and the older son will be his servant.”
text: Le Uwumbɔr nan bui u ke, “Ŋinibol ŋilee le bi aaponn ni; saah ga maa bijaam bi na ga li ye ŋinibol ŋilee aanib la, le ubaa ga li pɔɔ jer uken, upol le ga nan li tun tii uwaatiir.”

ref: the first baby was covered with red hair, so he was named Esau.
text: Njan aabo nan man le ki kpa tikor, nima pu na, bi nan yin u ke Esɔ.

ref: As Jacob and Esau grew older, Esau liked the outdoors and became a good hunter, while Jacob lived the quiet life of a shepherd.
text: Mbim ngbaan nan muun ki ŋa binib, le Esɔ nan nyi kikpaŋ aatuln pam, le ki nan gee chaa timoor ni sakpen, le Jakob nan ye unii u bi suuna na, le ki nan gee u li bi linampal.

ref: Esau would take the meat of wild animals to his father Isaac, so Isaac loved him more, but Jacob was his mother's favorite son.
text: Esɔ aah nan ye ukpan ki ku ipeel tii ute Aisak pu na, le u nan gee u pam, le Rebeka mu nan gee Jakob.

ref: One day, when Jacob was cooking some stew, Esau came home hungry
text: Daalbaadaal le Jakob nan bi ŋaa ŋituun aakpin, le Esɔ nyan ni kikpaŋ, le nkon joo u sakpen,

ref: and said, “I'm starving to death! Here and now give me some of that red stew!” That's how Esau got the name “Edom.”
text: le u bui Jakob ke, “M ga kpo nkon, tii mi saakpimann ngbaan siib m wub.” Nima pu le bi nan ki yin u ke Edom.

ref: Jacob replied, “Sell me your rights as the first-born son.”
text: Jakob nan giin bui u ke, “Di tininkpir tii mi aan m mu tii si tikpin ngbaan.”

ref: “I'm about to die,” Esau answered. “What good will those rights do me?”
text: Esɔ nan bui u ke, “Tɔ, nkon ban mu ku mi la, tininkpir na ga ŋa mi ba?”

ref: But Jacob said, “Promise me your birthrights, here and now!” And that's what Esau did.
text: Jakob nan bui u ke, “Pue puu tipuur ke aa ga di tininkpir ngbaan tii mi.” Esɔ nan puu tii Jakob le ki di tininkpir ngbaan kooh tii u.

ref: Jacob then gave Esau some bread and some of the bean stew, and when Esau had finished eating and drinking, he just got up and left, showing how little he thought of his rights as the first-born.
text: Le Jakob mu di tijikaar ni tikpin ngbaan tii Esɔ. U nan jin ki nyun le ki fii buen. Nima le mɔk ke Esɔ aatafal aa nan bi tininkpir ni.

ref: Once during Abraham's lifetime, the fields had not produced enough grain, and now the same thing happened. So Isaac went to King Abimelech of the Philistines in the land of Gerar,
text: Abraham aah nan bi Kanaann aatiŋ ni buyoonn na, le nkon mbaa nan lir nima, le Aisak aayoonn mu nkon nan ki lir kitiŋ ngbaan ni lelee. Aisak nan buen Abimelek, u nan ye Filistia yaab aabɔr na chee, Gerar aatiŋ ni.

ref: because the Lord had appeared to Isaac and said: Isaac, stay away from Egypt! I will show you where I want you to go.
text: Buyoonn waa nan kee buen Gerar aatiŋ ni na, le Uwumbɔr nan dan u chee ki nan bui u ke, “Taa buen Ijipt aatiŋ ni. Li bi kitiŋ kee ni, nin chee m bui si ke aa li bi na.

ref: You will live there as a foreigner, but I will be with you and bless you. I will keep my promise to your father Abraham by giving this land to you and your descendants.
text: Li koo do, le m ga li bi aa chee, ki ŋa tinyoor ŋa aa pu. M ban ke m di kitiŋ kee mɔmɔk tii sin ni aayaabitiib la. Maah nan puu tipuur ti tii aate Abraham na, m ga li joo ti.

ref: I will give you as many descendants as there are stars in the sky, and I will give your descendants all of this land. They will be a blessing to every nation on earth,
text: M ga cha aa maa ki li kpa yaabitiib bi li wiir ke iŋmabi na, le m ga di kitiŋ kee mɔmɔk tii bi. Aayaabitiib pu le m ga ŋa tinyoor ŋa ŋinibol mɔmɔk pu.

ref: After Isaac had been there a long time, King Abimelech looked out a window and saw Isaac hugging and kissing Rebekah.
text: Aisak aah nan bi nima ki yunn siib na, le ubɔr Abimelek nan bi waadiik ni, ki lik takolu ni, le ki kan Aisak ŋani Rebeka ke upuu na.

ref: Abimelech called him in and said, “Rebekah must be your wife! Why did you say she is your sister?” “Because I thought someone would kill me,” Isaac answered.
text: Le Abimelek tun bi ti yin ni Aisak, le ki nan baa u ke, “Rebeka ye aapuu la aa? Ba ŋa aa bui ke u ye aaninkpan la?” U nan giin bui ubɔr ngbaan ke, “M nan dak ke m yaa bui ke u ye mpuu kan, ni ga ku mi ki yoor u la.”

ref: “Don't you know what you've done?” Abimelech exclaimed. “If someone had slept with her, you would have made our whole nation guilty!”
text: Le Abimelek baa ke, “Ba ŋa aa ŋa timi kina? Saah nan len pu na, maanib ponn ni ubaa yaa ba doon u chee kan, titunwanbir ngbaan ba ga li ye saataani la.”

ref: Then Abimelech warned his people that anyone who even touched Isaac or Rebekah would be put to death.
text: Nima pu na, Abimelek nan sur waanib ke, “Ubaa taa ŋa Aisak ni upuu bakaa ubaa. Unii yaa ŋa bi bakaa kan, see bi ku udaan ngbaan.”

ref: and Isaac was so successful that he became very rich.
text: Waawan mɔmɔk nan too pɔr kpae, le u ŋa uwankpadaan.

ref: In fact, the Philistines were jealous of the large number of sheep, goats, and slaves that Isaac owned,
text: U nan kpa ipiih, ni iŋoob, ni inaa, ni binaagbiib sakpen, le Filistia yaab nan kpa u pu lipiipoln.

ref: and they stopped up the wells that Abraham's servants had dug before his death.
text: Nima pu le bi nan faa titan gbiin inyunbun imɔk ute Abraham aanaagbiib nan gbii buyoonn u nan bi na.

ref: Finally, Abimelech said, “Isaac, I want you to leave our country. You have become too powerful to stay here.”
text: Le Abimelek bui Aisak ke, “Nya timi aatiŋ ponn ni. Aa kan liwankpal jer timi sakpen a.”

ref: where he cleaned out those wells that the Philistines had stopped up. Isaac also gave each of the wells the same name that Abraham had given to them.
text: Ute Abraham aayoonn le bi nan gbii inyunbun. Abraham aakun aapuwɔb, le Filistia yaab nan faa titan gbiin i mɔmɔk, le Aisak ki lukr inyunbun ngbaan, ki yin i mɔmɔk ute aah nan yin i pu na.

ref: While his servants were digging in the valley, they found a spring-fed well.

ref: But the shepherds of Gerar Valley quarreled with Isaac's shepherds and claimed the water belonged to them. So this well was named “Quarrel,” because they had quarreled with Isaac.
text: Gerar aatiŋ aapihkpaab nan tak Aisak aapihkpaab, nnyunbun ngbaan pu, le ki bui ke, “Timi le yeh nnyunbun ngbaan.” Nima le cha Aisak nan yin nnyunbun ngbaan ke, “Kijaak.”

ref: Isaac's servants dug another well, and the shepherds also quarreled about it. So that well was named “Jealous.”
text: Le Aisak aapihkpaab nan ki gbii nnyunbun muken, le bi nan ki ban bi kijaak mu pu, nima le u nan yin mu ke, “Kinanaŋ.”

ref: Finally, they dug one more well. There was no quarreling this time, and the well was named “Lots of Room,” because the Lord had given them room and would make them very successful.
text: Le u fii nima chee ki buen ti ki gbii muken. Ubaa aa nan ki dan nan ban u kijaak mue pu, nima le u nan yin mu ke, “Lifuur.” U nan bui ke, “Dandana, Uwumbɔr tii timi lifuur ti li bi kitiŋ kee pu, ki kan tinyoor.”

ref: Isaac went on to Beersheba,

text: Kinyeek ngbaan le Uwumbɔr nan bui u tidaŋ ponn ni ke, “Min le ye aate Abraham Aawumbɔr. Taa li san ijawaan; m bi aa chee. M ga ŋa tinyoor ŋa aa pu, ki cha aa maa pɔr, ki li kpa yaabitiib sakpen, ke maah nan puu tii maatutunn Abraham pu na.”

ref: Isaac built an altar there and worshiped the Lord. Then he set up camp, and his servants started digging a well.
text: Aisak nan maa libimbiln nima chee, le ki toor kitork ki doon Uwumbɔr. Nima chee le u nan kal, le waanaagbiib nan ki gbii nnyunbun muken. Aisak ni Abimelek aah nan ŋa kimɔbaan pu na

ref: Meanwhile, Abimelech had left Gerar and was taking his advisor Ahuzzath and his army commander Phicol to see Isaac.

ref: When they arrived, Isaac asked, “Why are you here? Didn't you send me away because you hated me?”
text: Le Aisak nan baa bi ke, “Ba ŋa ni dan m chee dandana wee? Maah nan bi nimi aatiŋ ni na, naa nan gee maabɔr, le ki bui mi ke m nya nimi aatiŋ ni.”

ref: They answered, “We now know for certain that the Lord is with you, and we have decided there needs to be a peace treaty between you and us. So let's make a solemn agreement
text: Bi nan giin bui u ke, “Dandana wee ti bee ke Uwumbɔr bi aa chee, le ti ban ke ti ni si ŋa kimɔbaan. Ti ban ke aa puu ke

text: saan ŋa timi bakaa ubaa, ke taah kaa nan ŋa si bakaa pu na. Ti nan ŋa si tibulchinn, le ki cha aa nyan tido nsuudoon ponn ni. Dandana wee, ni kpiir mɔk timi ke Uwumbɔr bi aa chee ki ŋa si tinyoor sakpen.”

ref: Isaac gave a big feast for them, and everyone ate and drank.

text: Naah woln kichakpiik ni na, u ni Aisak nan puu tipuur tii tɔb. Le Aisak nan chɔi bi, le bi gir kun kijɔtiik ni.

text: ki nan ŋa tijikaar nyaan ti m gee sakpen na tii mi, m ji. M yaa jin doo kan, m ga ŋa maakookoo aanyoor ŋa aa pu, aan ki nin kpo.”

text: Aa di buen ti tii u aan u ji, le u ga ŋa tinyoor ŋa aa pu, aan ki nin kpo.”

ref: “My brother Esau is a hairy man,” Jacob reminded her. “And I am not.
text: Jakob nan bui una ke, “Aa nyi ke nkpel Esɔ kpa tikor uwon pu pam, maawon joon la.

ref: If my father touches me and realizes I am trying to trick him, he will put a curse on me instead of giving me a blessing.”
text: Nibaakan nte ga meeh mi ki bee ke Esɔ aa ye, m ŋmann u la; m yaa ŋa kina kan, nte ga puun lii m pu, kaan ŋa tinyoor ŋa m pu.”

ref: So Jacob brought the meat to his mother, and she cooked the tasty food that his father liked.
text: Le Jakob buen le ki ti joo ni tiŋonann ngbaan, le una ŋa tijikaar nyaan ti ute gee sakpen na.

ref: Then she took Esau's best clothes and put them on Jacob.
text: Le Rebeka yoor Esɔ aawanpeenkaan nyaan, ni bi linampal na, le ki di peen Jakob,

ref: She also covered the smooth part of his hands and neck with goatskins
text: le ki di iŋogban na paan Jakob aaŋaal, ni usil pu,

ref: and gave him some bread and the tasty food she had cooked.
text: ki di tijikaar nyaan ngbaan, ni waah ŋa boroboro u na, tii Jakob.

ref: Jacob went to his father and said, “Father, here I am.” “Which one of my sons are you?” his father asked.
text: Le Jakob di buen ute chee, ki ti yin u ke, “Nte.” Le ute kii ki baa ke, “Njapɔɔn ulau ye?”

ref: Jacob replied, “I am Esau, your first-born, and I have done what you told me. Please sit up and eat the meat I have brought. Then you can give me your blessing.”
text: Le Jakob bui u ke, “Min, aajapɔpol Esɔ ye. Saah bui ke m ŋa pu na, m doo a. Fii kal ki ŋmɔ tinann ngbaan, aan ki ŋa tinyoor ŋa m pu.”

ref: Isaac asked, “My son, how did you find an animal so quickly?” “The Lord your God was kind to me,” Jacob answered.
text: Aisak nan baa u ke, “Maabo, aa ŋa kinye ki kan tinann ngbaan mala kina?” Jakob nan bui u ke, “Aadindaan Uwumbɔr le ter mi.”

ref: “My son,” Isaac said, “come closer, where I can touch you and find out if you really are Esau.”
text: Le Aisak bui Jakob ke, “Njapɔɔn, foor dan m chee, aan m di ŋŋaal baan aa pu. Mbamɔn, Esɔ sil ye ee?”

ref: Jacob went closer. His father touched him and said, “You sound like Jacob, but your hands feel hairy like Esau's.”
text: Le Jakob foor buen ute chee, le ute baan u pu le ki bui ke, “Nneel ngbaan naahn Jakob aaneel la, le ŋŋaal ma tee ye Esɔ aaŋaal.”

ref: Isaac asked, “Are you really my son Esau?” “Yes, I am,” Jacob answered.
text: le ki ki baa lelee ke, “Mbamɔn, Esɔ sil ye ee?” Le Jakob bui ke, “Min Esɔ ye.”

ref: So Isaac told him, “Serve me the wild meat, and I can give you my blessing.” Jacob gave him some meat, and he ate it. He also gave him some wine, and he drank it.
text: Le Aisak bui ke, “Njapɔɔn, li joo ni tinann ngbaan, m ŋmɔ. M yaa ŋman doo kan, m ga ŋa tinyoor ŋa aa pu.” Le Jakob di tinann ngbaan tii u, ki kpee ŋisubil aadaan, le u jin ki nyun.

ref: Then Isaac said, “Son, come over here and kiss me.”
text: Waah jin doo na, le u bui ke, “Maabo, foor dan m chee ki nan moor ntakpiln.”

ref: While Jacob was kissing him, Isaac caught the smell of his clothes and said: “The smell of my son is like a field the Lord has blessed.
text: Waah fuu ute chee buyoonn ki ban u moor utakpiln na, le Aisak ŋun waawanpeenkaan aabaan, ki ŋa tinyoor ŋa Jakob pu. U nan bui ke, “Maabo aabaan naahn kisaak ki Uwumbɔr ŋa tinyoor ŋa ki pu na la.

ref: God will bless you, my son, with dew from heaven and with fertile fields, rich with grain and grapes.
text: Uwumbɔr ga tii si limaln li nyan ni paacham na, ki cha saasaak ngbaan ŋa tijikaar sakpen. U cha aa li chee tijikaar ki li kpa ŋisubil aadaan sakpen.

ref: Nations will be your servants and bow down to you. You will rule over your brothers, and they will kneel at your feet. Anyone who curses you will be cursed; anyone who blesses you will be blessed.”
text: U cha ŋitimbol mɔmɔk aanib li ye saanaagbiib ki gbaan aanimbiin ni. U cha aa li ye aado yaab, ni aana aabim mɔmɔk aayidaan, aan bi li gbaani aanimbiin ni. Uwumbɔr puun lii unii umɔk puun lii aa pu na pu, aan ki ŋa tinyoor ŋa unii umɔk ŋa si tiŋann na pu.” Esɔ aah nan gaŋ Aisak pu na aabɔr

ref: Right after Isaac had given Jacob his blessing and Jacob had gone, Esau came back from hunting.
text: Kina le Aisak nan ŋa tinyoor ŋa Jakob pu. Waah ti doo buyoonn, aan Jakob nyan buen na, le ukpel Esɔ mu nyan ni kikpaŋ kun ni.

ref: He cooked the tasty food, brought it to his father, and said, “Father, please sit up and eat the meat I have brought you, so you can give me your blessing.”
text: U mu nan ŋa tijikaar nyaan ti ute gee na, le ki di buen ute chee, ki nan bui u ke, “Tɔ, nte, fii kal ki ŋmɔ tinann ngbaan, nima kan, aa ga ŋa tinyoor ŋa m pu.”

ref: “Who are you?” Isaac asked. “I am Esau, your first-born son.”
text: Aisak nan baa ke, “Ŋma ye?” Le u bui ke, “Min, aajapɔpol Esɔ ye.”

ref: Isaac started trembling and said, “Then who brought me some wild meat right before you came in? I ate it and gave him a blessing that cannot be taken back.”
text: Le Aisak aah ŋun kina na, le uwon faa tɔtɔk, le u baa ke, “Ŋma ba ku ni upeel ki joo ni m chee na? M laa jin tima le doo le aa nin koo ni na. M di maakookoo aanyoor tii u a, nima pu na, udaan le yeh tinyoor ngbaan ki cha n‑yoonn mu kaa kpa ndoon na.”

ref: Esau cried out in great distress, “Father, give me a blessing too!”
text: Esɔ aah ŋun kina na, le usui bii, le u wii ki kaani sakpen ki bui ke, “Nte, ŋa tinyoor ŋa m mu pu.”

ref: Isaac answered, “Your brother tricked me and stole your blessing.”
text: Ute Aisak nan bui u ke, “Aanaal Jakob dan nan ŋmann mi ki gaa saanyoor a.”

ref: Esau replied, “My brother deserves the name Jacob, because he has already cheated me twice. The first time he cheated me out of my rights as the first-born son, and now he has cheated me out of my blessing.” Then Esau asked his father, “Don't you have any blessing left for me?”
text: Le Esɔ bui ke, “U jin mi nfum mulee le na. Mbamɔn, ni ŋeer bi yin u ke Jakob. Njan u nan ŋmann mi ki gaa tininkpir, le ki ki ŋmann tite mu ki gaa maanyoor. Nte, saa gur tinyoor tibaa ki ŋa m pu uu?”

ref: “My son,” Isaac answered, “I have made Jacob the ruler over you and your brothers, and all of you will be his servants. I have also promised him all the grain and grapes that he needs. There's nothing left that I can do for you.”
text: Le Aisak bui u ke, “M di tininkpir tii uma la, le u ga li ye aa pu usaloln, le udo yaab mɔmɔk ga li ye waanaagbiib. Le u ga li kpa tijikaar ki kpa ŋisubil aadaan mu sakpen. Maabo, tinyoor tibaa aa ki bi m ga ki ŋa aa pu.”

ref: “Father,” Esau asked, “don't you have more than one blessing? You can surely give me a blessing too!” Then Esau started crying again.
text: Le Esɔ nan bi ki gaŋni ute ki bui u ke, “Nte, tinyoor tibaa aa gur aa? Nte, ŋa tinyoor ŋa m mu pu.” Usui aah bii sakpen pu na, le u wii ki kaani.

ref: So his father said: “Your home will be far from that fertile land, where dew comes down from the heavens.
text: Le ute Aisak bui u ke, “Limaln aan nyan ni paacham ki lir ni aa pu. Kitiŋ aan li kpa tinyoor tii sin.

ref: You will live by the power of your sword and be your brother's slave. But when you decide to be free, you will break loose.”
text: Saajawan pu le aa ga li bi. Aa ga li ye aanaal aanaagbiija la. Le aa yaa yii kan, aa ga nyan uŋaal ni.”

ref: Esau hated his brother Jacob because he had stolen the blessing that was supposed to be his. So he said to himself, “Just as soon as my father dies, I'll kill Jacob.”
text: Esɔ nan nann Jakob, ba pu? ute nan ŋa tinyoor ŋa Jakob pu ki cha u. U nan dakl ke, “Nte aakpotoor aa ki daa, waakpuul yaa jer kan, le m ga ku Jakob.”

ref: When Rebekah found out what Esau planned to do, she sent for Jacob and told him, “Son, your brother Esau is just waiting for a chance to kill you.
text: Tɔ, bina Rebeka aah nan ŋun Esɔ aah dak ki len pu na, le u yin Jakob, ki bui u ke, “Li pel, aakpel Esɔ bi dakl waah ga ŋa pu ki ku si na.

ref: Now listen carefully and do what I say. Go to the home of my brother Laban in Haran
text: Maabo, ŋa maah ban m bui si pu na. Siir mala ki san buen nninja Labann do, Harann aatiŋ ni,

ref: and stay with him for a while. When Esau stops being angry
text: ki ti li bi nima, ki kii buyoonn aakpel Esɔ aaŋuul ga ti sɔŋ na,

ref: and forgets what you have done to him, I'll send for you to come home. Why should I lose both of my sons on the same day?”
text: aan u yaa suln saah ŋa pu na kan, le m ga tun ni bi nan yin sin. Naa ŋan ke m kpaan lann njapɔtiib bilee kina liwinbaal.” Aisak aah nan cha Jakob san buen Labann do pu na aabɔr

ref: Rebekah later told Isaac, “Those Hittite wives of Esau are making my life miserable! If Jacob marries a Hittite woman, I'd be better off dead.”
text: Le Rebeka bui uchal Aisak ke, “Esɔ aapuutiib aabimbin aah bi pu na muk mi sakpen, ki cha naa mɔ mi n‑yoonn mɔmɔk. Jakob mu yaa ki yoor Hef yaab aapiibo ubaa kan, naan ki li mɔ kpala.”

ref: Isaac called in Jacob, then gave him a blessing, and said: Don't marry any of those Canaanite women.
text: Aisak nan yin Jakob, le ki doon u, le ki bui u, “Taa yoor Kanaann aatiŋ kee ni aapiibo.

ref: Go at once to your mother's father Bethuel in northern Syria and choose a wife from one of the daughters of Laban, your mother's brother.
text: Li cha Mesopotamia aatiŋ ni, aayaaja Betuel do, ki ti yoor nima chee aapiibo, aaweeja Labann aabisatiib ponn ni ubaa.

ref: I pray that God All-Powerful will bless you with many descendants and let you become a great nation.
text: Uwumbɔr Mpɔɔnsakpiindaan ŋa tinyoor ŋa aa ni aapuu pu, ki cha ni maa pɔr sakpen, nima kan, aa ga li ye ŋitimbol pam aate.

ref: May he bless you with the land he promised Abraham, so that you will take over this land where we now live as foreigners.
text: Uwumbɔr ŋa tinyoor ŋa aa ni aayaabitiib pu, ke waah ŋa tinyoor ŋa aayaaja Abraham pu pu na, aan ki cha aa gaa kitiŋ ki u nan di tii aayaaja Abraham aan aa bi ki ponn ni na.”

ref: Isaac then sent Jacob to stay with Rebekah's brother Laban, the son of Bethuel the Aramean. Esau Marries the Daughter of Ishmael
text: Kina le Aisak nan cha Jakob buen Mesopotamia aatiŋ ni, uweeja Labann do. Labann nan ye Aram aatiŋ aanii Betuel aajapɔɔn, ki mu ye Jakob ni Esɔ aana Rebeka aaninja la. Esɔ aah nan ki yoor upii uken pu na aabɔr

ref: Esau found out that his father Isaac had blessed Jacob and had warned him not to marry any of the Canaanite women. He also learned that Jacob had been sent to find a wife in northern Syria
text: Esɔ nan ŋun ke ute Aisak ŋa tinyoor ŋa Jakob pu, le ki bui u ke u li cha Mesopotamia aatiŋ ni, ki ti ban upii nima chee. U nan ki ŋun ke Aisak aah ŋa tinyoor ŋa u pu na, u nan sur u ke u taa yoor Kanaann aapiibo.

ref: and that he had obeyed his father and mother.
text: U nan bee ke Jakob kii ute ni una aamɔb ki buen Mesopotamia aatiŋ ni.

ref: Esau already had several wives, but he now realized how much his father hated the Canaanite women.
text: Esɔ nan bee ke ute Aisak aa gee ke bi yoor Kanaann aapiibim.

ref: So he married Ishmael's daughter Mahalath, who was the sister of Nebaioth and the granddaughter of Abraham. Jacob's Dream at Bethel
text: Nima pu na, u nan buen Ismael, u nan ye Abraham aajapɔɔn na chee, le ki ti yoor ubisal Mahalaf u nan ye Nebayof aaninkpan na, ki di kpee upuutiib biken na pu. Jakob aah nan daŋ tidaŋ pu na aabɔr

ref: Jacob left the town of Beersheba and started out for Haran.
text: Tɔ, Jakob nan nyan Beerseba le ki nan cha Harann wɔb.

ref: At sunset he stopped for the night and went to sleep, resting his head on a large rock.
text: Nwiin aah nan ti lir na, le u fuu nibaa chee le ki doon nima. U nan doon ke u geen, le ki di uyil kur litakpal libaa.

ref: In a dream he saw a ladder that reached from earth to heaven, and God's angels were going up and down on it.
text: U nan doon daŋ ke u kan mpijadɔ si kitiŋ ki ti tuu paacham, le Uwumbɔr aatuuntiib chuun mu pu ki cha paacham ki ki sunni.

ref: The Lord was standing beside the ladder and said: I am the Lord God who was worshiped by Abraham and Isaac. I will give to you and your family the land on which you are now sleeping.
text: Le u kan ke Uwumbɔr si gbɔk u ki bui u ke, “Min le ye Uwumbɔr, aayaaja Abraham ni aate Aisak Aawumbɔr. M ga di kitiŋ ki aa dɔ ki pu na tii aa ni aayaabitiib.

ref: Your descendants will spread over the earth in all directions and will become as numerous as the specks of dust. Your family will be a blessing to all people.
text: Bi ga li wiir ke kitiŋ aatanbol na. Bi ga yaa gbiin dulnyaa wee ni mɔmɔk. Le sin pu, ni aayaabitiib pu, m ga ŋa tinyoor ŋa ŋinibol mɔmɔk pu.

ref: Wherever you go, I will watch over you, then later I will bring you back to this land. I won't leave you—I will do all I have promised.
text: Taa suln, ki li teer n‑yoonn mɔmɔk ke m ga li bi aa chee, le ki ga li joo si saah ga buen nimɔk na, ki ga ki giin ni si kitiŋ kee pu. Maan di si lii see m ŋa maah puu ke m ga ŋa si pu na.”

ref: Jacob woke up suddenly and thought, “The Lord is in this place, and I didn't even know it.”
text: Le Jakob nan finn ngeen pu, ki bui ke, “Uwumbɔr bi do. U bi do chee, le maa nyi kina.”

ref: Then Jacob became frightened and said, “What a frightening place! It must be the house of God and the gateway to heaven.”
text: U nan san ijawaan le ki bui ke, “Do chee na kpa ijawaan sakpen a. Uwumbɔr do sil sɔ, ni ga sil li ye nin chee bi piir koo paacham na la.”

ref: When Jacob got up early the next morning, he took the rock that he had used for a pillow and stood it up as a place of worship. Then he poured olive oil on the rock to dedicate it to God,
text: Naah chaa woln ni na, le Jakob pue fii kichakpiik ni, le ki yoor litakpal li u nan di uyil kur na, le ki di ŋum nima chee aan li li ye litakpateerkaan. U nan di nkpan kpir litakpal ngbaan pu ki di li tii Uwumbɔr,

ref: and he named the place Bethel. Before that it had been named Luz.
text: le ki yin nima chee ke Betel. Bi nan yin kitiŋ ngbaan n‑yoonn mubaa ke Lus.

ref: Jacob solemnly promised God, “If you go with me and watch over me as I travel, and if you give me food and clothes
text: Le u nan puu tipuur nima chee ki tii Uwumbɔr ke, “Sin Uwumbɔr yaa ga li bi m chee, ki joo mi maasachuln ni, ki ga tii mi tijikaar ni tiwanpeenkaan,

ref: and bring me safely home again, you will be my God.
text: ki cha m gir kun nte do laafee kan, sin le ga li ye Maawumbɔr.

ref: As Jacob continued on his way to the east,
text: Jakob nan fii Betel aatiŋ ni, le ki nab waasachuln pu ki cha liwipuul wɔb.

ref: he looked out in a field and saw a well where shepherds took their sheep for water. Three flocks of sheep were lying around the well, which was covered with a large rock.
text: Waah cha na, le u ti kpaan nyan nnyunbun mubaa pu, le ki kan ipiih ŋiker ŋitaa pu dɔ mann goln nnyunbun ngbaan. Bipihkpaab bi nan joo ipiih ngbaan na nan lu nnyunbun ngbaan aanyun le tii ipiih ngbaan le i nyu, le bi nan di litakpasakpeln libaa di biin nnyunbun ngbaan aamɔb.

ref: Shepherds would roll the rock away when all their sheep had gathered there. Then after the sheep had been watered, the shepherds would roll the rock back over the mouth of the well.
text: Bipihkpaab ngbaan mɔmɔk yaa nan ti joo ni baapiih kan, le bi chuu biln litakpal ngbaan le ki lu nnyun tii i, le ki ki giin litakpal na di biin mu pu.

ref: Jacob asked the shepherds, “Where are you from?” “We're from Haran,” they answered.
text: Jakob nan baa bipihkpaab ngbaan ke, “Njɔtiib, ni nyan ni la chee?” Le bi bui ke, “Ti nyan ni Harann aatiŋ ni la.”

ref: Then he asked, “Do you know Nahor's grandson Laban?” “Yes we do,” they replied.
text: Le u baa bi ke, “Ni nyi Labann, u ye Nahor aajapɔɔn na aa?” Le bi bui u ke, “Een, ti nyi u.”

ref: “How is he?” Jacob asked. “He's fine,” they answered. “And here comes his daughter Rachel with the sheep.”
text: Jakob nan ki baa bi ke, “U pɔɔ ɔɔ?” Le bi bui u ke, “U pɔɔ. Ubisal Rachel le joo ute aapiih choo na.”

ref: Jacob told them, “Look, the sun is still high up in the sky, and it's too early to bring in the rest of the flocks. Water your sheep and take them back to the pasture.”
text: Le Jakob bui ke, “Nwiin aah laa wiir sakpen, kaa kee ŋeer ni di ipiih kunn na, ba ŋa naan piir nnyunbun aan ki lu nnyun tii ipiih ki ki giin i buen timoor ni?”

ref: But they replied, “We can't do that until they all get here, and the rock has been rolled away from the well.”
text: Le bi giin bui u ke, “Taan ŋmaa ŋa kina, see ipiih ngbaan mɔmɔk fuu ni waahr le ti nin piir ki tii i nnyun.”

ref: While Jacob was still talking with the men, his cousin Rachel came up with her father's sheep.
text: Buyoonn Jakob nan bi len bi chee tibɔr ngbaan na, le Rachel fuu ni ki joo ni ute aapiih. U nan ye upihkpaal la.

ref: When Jacob saw her and his uncle's sheep, he rolled the rock away and watered the sheep.
text: Jakob aah nan kan Rachel joo ni uweeja Labann aapiih na, le u buen nnyunbun na chee, ki ti chuu piir litakpal ngbaan ki lun nnyun tii ipiih ngbaan.

ref: He then kissed Rachel and started crying because he was so happy.
text: Le u buen ti moor Rachel aatakpiln ki piin ki kaani mpopiin pu.

ref: He told her that he was the son of her aunt Rebekah, and she ran and told her father about him.
text: U nan tuk Rachel ke, “M ye aate aamaal ni aanii la, Rebeka le ye nna.” Rachel nan san buen ti tuk ute;

ref: As soon as Laban heard the news, he ran out to meet Jacob. He hugged and kissed him and brought him to his home, where Jacob told him everything that had happened.
text: le ute aah ŋun uweeja Jakob aabɔr na, u nan san tooh u nsan pu ki ti bub chuu u ki moor utakpiln, le ki di u buen udo. Jakob nan tuk u naah ŋa pu, u ti dan u chee na.

ref: Laban said, “You are my nephew, and you are like one of my own family.” Jacob Marries Leah and Rachel After Jacob had been there for a month,
text: Le uma Labann bui u ke, “Een, mbamɔn, aa sil ye maawon, ni maasin ni aanii la.” Le Jakob nan bi u chee ki ti saa uŋmal ubaa. Jakob aah nan yoor Rachel ni Lea pu na aabɔr

ref: Laban said to him, “You shouldn't have to work without pay, just because you are a relative of mine. What do you want me to give you?”
text: Labann nan bui Jakob ke, “Naa mɔ ke aa li tun lituln tii mi dafir, ke saah ye maanii na pu. Aa ban ke m pa si iŋa?”

ref: Laban had two daughters. Leah was older than Rachel, but her eyes didn't sparkle, while Rachel was beautiful and had a good figure.
text: Lea aanimbil nan ŋan pam, Rachel mu nan tee ŋan sakpen ki jer u.

ref: Since Jacob was in love with Rachel, he answered, “If you will let me marry Rachel, I'll work seven years for you.”
text: Jakob nan gee Rachel sakpen, nima pu le u bui ke, “Aa yaa ga di Rachel tii mi kan, m ga tun lituln ki ti saa ŋibin ŋilole tii si.”

ref: Laban replied, “It's better for me to let you marry Rachel than for someone else to have her. So stay and work for me.”
text: Le Labann bui u ke, “M yaa di u tii si kan, ni ŋan ki jer m di u tii unii ubaa, kina pu na, li bi m chee ki tun tii mi.”

ref: Jacob worked seven years for Laban, but the time seemed like only a few days, because he loved Rachel so much.
text: Le Jakob nan tun ki ti saa ŋibin ŋilole ngbaan ke u gaa Rachel, le waah nan gee Rachel sakpen pu na, ŋibin ŋilole ngbaan aa nan yunn u chee.

ref: Jacob said to Laban, “The time is up, and I want to marry Rachel now!”
text: N‑yoonn ngbaan aah fuu na, le Jakob bui Labann ke, “N‑yoonn ngbaan fuu a, di aabisal tii mi.”

ref: But that evening he brought Leah to Jacob, who married her and spent the night with her.
text: Kinyeek ngbaan le Labann nan di Lea koon Jakob aadiik ni ti tii u, le bi kpaan tɔb chee.

ref: Laban also gave Zilpah to Leah as her servant woman.
text: Labann nan di waanaagbiipiibo Silpa di tii ubisal Lea, u li tun waatuln.

ref: The next morning Jacob found out that he had married Leah, and he asked Laban, “Why did you do this to me? Didn't I work to get Rachel? Why did you trick me?”
text: Naah woln kichakpiik ni na, le Jakob bee ke ni ye Lea le u ni u fe kpaan kinyeek na. Le u buen Labann chee ki ti bui u ke, “Ba ŋa aa ŋa mi kina? M nan choon si ki ban Rachel la. Ba ŋa aa ŋmann mi?”

ref: Laban replied, “In our country the older daughter must get married first.
text: Le Labann nan bui u ke, “Ni ye timi aakaal le ke see ti pue di upiibopol tii tichal, le ki nin paan uwaatiir.

ref: After you spend this week with Leah, you may also marry Rachel. But you will have to work for me another seven years.”
text: Cha likpaakool libaa jer waahr, aan saah yoor Lea pu na aasariyaa yaa jer kan, m ki di Rachel mu tii si; le aa ga ki tun lituln tii mi ŋibin ŋilole u mu pu.”

ref: At the end of the week of celebration, Laban let Jacob marry Rachel, and he gave her his servant woman Bilhah. Jacob loved Rachel more than he did Leah, but he had to work another seven years for Laban.
text: Jakob nan ki yoor Rachel mu u li ye upuu, le u nan gee u ki jer Lea. Rachel pu na, u nan ki tun lituln ki ti saa ŋibin ŋilole ki tii Labann. Jakob aah nan ma mbim pu na aabɔr

ref: The Lord knew that Jacob loved Rachel more than he did Leah, and so he gave children to Leah, but not to Rachel.
text: Uwumbɔr aah nan kan ke Jakob gee Rachel ki jer Lea na, le u nan cha Lea pue ma mbim, le Rachel nan chuun kaa maa.

ref: Leah gave birth to a son and named him Reuben. Then she said, “The Lord has taken away my sorrow. Now my husband will love me more than he does Rachel.”
text: Lea nan dek lipuul, le ki ma ubijabo. Le u bui ke, “Uwumbɔr kan maamukl. Maah ma ubijabo wee na, nchal ga li gee mi.” Nima pu na, u nan yin ubo ngbaan ke Rubenn.

ref: She had a second son and named him Simeon, because she said, “The Lord has heard that my husband doesn't love me.”
text: U nan ki dek lipuul ki ki ma ubijabo, ki bui ke, “Uwumbɔr ŋun ke nchal aa gee mi, le u ki tii mi ubijabo wee mu.” Nima pu na, u nan yin u ke Simeonn.

ref: When Leah's third son was born, she said, “Now my husband will hold me close.” So this son was named Levi.
text: Le u nan ki dek taataa ki ki ma ubijabo uken. U ki bui ke, “Dandana wee, maah ki ma ubijabo tatar tii nchal na kan, u ga moo joo mi mbamɔm.” Nima pu na, u nan yin u ke Liifai.

ref: Rachel was very jealous of Leah for having children, and she said to Jacob, “I'll die if you don't give me some children!”
text: Tɔ, Rachel aa nan ma ubo tii Jakob, nima pu na, u nan kpa ukpel Lea pu lipiipoln, le ki bui uchal Jakob ke, “Aa yaa kaa tii mi mbim kan, m ga kpo.”

ref: But Jacob became upset with Rachel and answered, “Don't blame me! I'm not God.”
text: Jakob aaŋuul nan fii Rachel pu pam, le u bui u ke, “Maa ye Uwumbɔr ki gaa waatuln tun tii u. Uma le aa tii si mbim.”

ref: “Here, take my servant Bilhah,” Rachel told him. “Have children by her, and I'll let them be born on my knees to show that they are mine.”
text: Le Rachel nan bui uchal ke, “Maanaagbiipii Bilha sɔ. Doon u chee, nima kan, u ga maa ubo tii mi. Aa yaa ŋa kina kan, u ga maa ubo tii mi, le m li kpa mbim.”

ref: and they had a son.
text: Le Bilha nan dek lipuul ki ma ubijabo tii Jakob.

ref: Rachel named him Dan, because she said, “God has answered my prayers. He judged in my favor and has given me a son.”
text: Rachel nan bui ke, “Uwumbɔr le toor maabɔr tii mi. Uma le ŋun maameel le ki tii mi ubijabo.” Nima pu na, u nan yin u ke Dann.

ref: When Bilhah and Jacob had a second son,
text: Le Bilha nan ki dek lipuul, le ki ki ma ubijabo uken tii Jakob.

ref: Rachel said, “I've struggled hard with my sister, and I've won!” So she named the boy Naphtali.
text: Rachel nan ki bui ke, “M pɔɔn mbaa ki mee Uwumbɔr ki kan mbim ke nkpel aah kan pu na.” Nima pu na, u nan yin u ke Naftali.

ref: When Leah realized she could not have any more children, she let Jacob marry her servant Zilpah,
text: Le Lea kan ke waa ki maa, le u di waanaagbiipiibo Silpa ki di tii uchal Jakob u di u ŋa upuu.

ref: and they had a son.
text: Le Silpa nan ma ubijabo tii Jakob.

ref: “I'm really lucky,” Leah said, and she named the boy Gad.
text: Lea nan bui ke, “M kpa liyimɔɔn.” Nima pu na, u nan yin u ke Gad.

ref: When they had another son,
text: Silpa nan ki ma ubijabo uken tii Jakob.

ref: Leah exclaimed, “I'm happy now, and all the women will say how happy I am.” So she named him Asher. Love Flowers
text: Le Lea bui ke, “M kpa mpopiin pam. Dandana wee, bipiib biken ga yin mi ke mpopiindaan.” Nima pu na, u nan yin u ke Aser.

ref: During the time of the wheat harvest, Reuben found some love flowers and took them to his mother Leah. Rachel asked Leah for some of them,
text: Lidicheel yoonn nan fuu, le Rubenn buen tisar ponn ni ki ti ban ni ŋisubil ŋibaa, bi yin ŋi ke ngeehm aasubil, le ki joo ni ŋi nan tii una Lea. Le Rachel bui Lea ke, “Tii mi aajapɔɔn aasubil ngbaan ŋibaa.”

ref: but Leah said, “It's bad enough that you stole my husband! Now you want my son's love flowers too.” “All right,” Rachel answered. “Let me have the flowers, and you can sleep with Jacob tonight.”
text: Lea nan giin bui u ke, “Saah gaa m chee nchal pu na aa ŋeer aa? Dandana wee aa ki pɔɔni aabaa ke aa gaa njapɔɔn aasubil mu la aa?” Rachel nan bui u ke, “Aa yaa kii ki ga tii mi ŋisubil ngbaan kan, m ga di nchal tii si kinyeek kee, aa ni u doon.”

ref: That evening when Jacob came in from the fields, Leah told him, “You're sleeping with me tonight. I hired you with my son's love flowers.” They slept together that night,
text: Tɔ, buyoonn Jakob nan nyan ni kisaak ki kun ni kijook wɔb na, le Lea buen u chee ki ti bui u ke, “Kinyeek kee, aa ga koo ni maadiik ni la; ba pu? m di njapɔɔn aasubil pan n‑yɔnn, aa pu a.” Nima pu na, Jakob nan koo Lea aadiik ni kinyeek ngbaan, le ki doon u chee.

ref: and God answered Leah's prayers by giving her a fifth son.
text: Uwumbɔr nan ŋun Lea aameel pu, le u dek lipuul ki ki ma ubijabo ŋmuŋmur tii Jakob.

ref: Leah shouted, “God has rewarded me for letting Jacob marry my servant,” and she named the boy Issachar.
text: Le Lea bui ke, “Uwumbɔr pan mi maapar a, ba pu? m di maanaagbiipiibo tii nchal.” Nima pu na, u nan yin waabo ngbaan ke Isakar.

ref: she exclaimed, “God has given me a wonderful gift, and my husband will praise me for giving him six sons.” So she named the boy Zebulun.
text: ki nan bui ke, “Uwumbɔr tii mi ipiin nyanyaan. Dandana wee, nchal ga nyuŋ mi, ba pu? m ki ma ubijabo loloob tii u.” Nima pu na, u nan yin u ke Sebulunn.

ref: Later, Leah had a daughter and named her Dinah.
text: Le ni joo cha, le u nan ki ma upiibo mu, le ki yin u ke Daina.

ref: Finally, God remembered Rachel—he answered her prayer by giving her a son. “God has taken away my disgrace,” she said.
text: U nan dek lipuul le ki ma ubijabo. Le u bui ke, “Uwumbɔr chuu nyan maanimɔɔn le ki tii mi ubijabo.

ref: “I'll name the boy Joseph, and I'll pray that the Lord will give me another son.” Jacob and Laban
text: Cha Uwumbɔr ki tii mi ubijabo.” Nima pu le u yin u ke Josef. Jakob ni Labann aah nan kpokl pu na aabɔr

ref: After Joseph was born, Jacob said to Laban, “Release me from our agreement and let me return to my own country.
text: Rachel aah nan ma Josef na, le Jakob bui Labann ke, “Cha m li chaa kun, ki gir buen ndo.

ref: You know how hard I've worked for you, so let me take my wives and children and leave.”
text: Di mpuutiib ni maabim bi m tun tii si bi pu na tii mi aan m kun. Aa nyi maah tun litunyaan pu ki tii si na.”

ref: Jacob answered: You've seen how hard I've worked for you, and you know how your flocks and herds have grown under my care.
text: Le Jakob bui ke, “Aa nyi maah tun lituln tii si pu, ki joo saapiih i pɔr sakpen pu na.

ref: You didn't have much before I came, but the Lord has blessed everything I have ever done for you. Now it's time for me to start looking out for my own family.
text: Saapiih siib i aa nan kpa aan m fuu ni na, aa kan ke i pɔr sakpen, le m yaa di i buen nimɔk na kan, Uwumbɔr ŋani tinyoor ŋani i pu. Dandana wee, n‑yoonn fuu ni ke m mu ban mbaa yaan.”

ref: “How much do you want me to pay you?” Laban asked. Then Jacob told him: I don't want you to pay me anything. Just do one thing, and I'll take care of your sheep and goats.
text: Le Labann baa u ke, “Aa ban ke m pa si ba?” Le Jakob bui u ke, “Maa ban tipar tibaa. M ga li beenin kpaa saapiih ngbaan, see aa ti kii maah ban m kpokl si pu na.

ref: Let me go through your flocks and herds and take the sheep and goats that are either spotted or speckled and the black lambs. That's all you need to give me.
text: Cha m lik saapiih ngbaan ponn ni, ki nyan ipiih imɔk kpa mbɔm na, ni imɔk kpa mpem ni mbɔm na, ni iŋoob imɔk kpa mpem ni mbɔm na. Maah ban tipar ti na le na.

ref: In the future you can easily find out if I've been honest. Just look and see if my animals are either spotted or speckled, or if the lambs are black. If they aren't, they've been stolen from you.
text: Ni yaa bi cha kan, aa lik ke m ye unibamɔnn aan maa ye. Buyoonn aa ga nan lik i na, aa yaa kan ipiih i kaa kpa mbɔm, bee iŋoob i kaa kpa mpem ni mbɔm na i ponn ni kan, aa bee ke m sun i la.”

ref: Before the end of the day, Laban had separated his spotted and speckled animals and the black lambs from the others and had put his sons in charge of them.
text: Tɔ, liyaadaal ngbaan le Labann nan nyan iŋoja i ye ichachal na, ni iŋoninkpel i mu ye ichachal, bee i kpa mpem i pu na ibaa. U nan ki nyan ipihbɔn mu mɔmɔk le ki di i mɔmɔk ŋa ujapɔtiib aaŋaal ni,

ref: Then Laban made Jacob keep the rest of the sheep and goats at a distance of three days' journey.
text: ki cha bi chuun iwiin itaa ki buen timoor ni, nin chee daa na. Le Jakob nan gaa Labann aapiih i gur na ki joo i.

ref: Jacob cut branches from some poplar trees and from some almond and evergreen trees. He peeled off part of the bark and made the branches look spotted and speckled.
text: Jakob nan ban isui ibaa aaboln aabon, le ki ban isui i aawon joon na mu aabon, le ki chuu puur tidapɔr i pu.

ref: Then he put the branches where the sheep and goats would see them while they were drinking from the water trough. The goats mated there
text: U nan di ibon ngbaan le ki bil tiwakor ngbaan aah nyu nnyun nin chee na. U nan di ibon ngbaan di bil nnyun chee, ba pu? buyoonn tiwakor dan ti nan nyu nnyun na kan, le ija dur isal pu.

ref: Some of the sheep that Jacob was keeping for Laban were already spotted. And when the others were ready to mate, he made sure that they faced in the direction of the spotted and black ones. In this way, Jacob built up a flock of sheep for himself and did not put them with the other sheep.
text: Jakob nan ki yakr iŋoob ni ipiih tɔb chee, le ki cha ti sil toŋ Labann aawakor ti ye tichachal ni tibɔmbɔnn na wɔb. U nan ŋa kina ke uma aawakor pɔr kpee, le ki yakr ti Labann yaar chee.

ref: When the stronger sheep were mating near the drinking place, Jacob made sure that the spotted branches were there.
text: Tiwakor ti nan kpa laafee na yaa ti ban ti dur tɔb pu kan, le Jakob di ibon ngbaan di bil yaah nyu nnyun nin chee na, nima kan, i ga maa ibɔnbɔn ni ichachal,

ref: But he would not put out the branches when the weaker animals were mating. So Jacob got all of the healthy animals, and Laban got what was left.
text: le kaa nan di ibon ngbaan di bil i kaa kpa laafee na aanimbiin ni. Nima pu le Labann aawakor ti nan gur na mɔmɔk aa kpa mpɔɔn, le Jakob yaar mɔmɔk ma nan kpa mpɔɔn.

ref: Jacob soon became rich and successful. He owned many sheep, goats, camels, and donkeys, as well as a lot of slaves.
text: Kina le Jakob nan ŋa mpɔɔndaan ki kpa tiwakor, ni binaagbiib, ni laakumii tiib, ni ibon sakpen.

ref: Jacob heard that Laban's sons were complaining, “Jacob is now a rich man, and he got everything he owns from our father.”
text: Le Jakob ŋun ke Labann aajapɔtiib bui ke, “Jakob gaa tite aawan mɔmɔk a. Waah kpa tiwan nimɔk na nyan ni tite yaan ponn ni la.”

ref: Jacob also noticed that Laban was not as friendly as he had been before.
text: Jakob nan ki kan ke uweeja Labann aa nan ki gee waabɔr ke waah nan gee u njan pu na.

ref: One day the Lord said, “Jacob, go back to your relatives in the land of your ancestors, and I will be with you.”
text: Le Uwumbɔr nan bui Jakob ke, “Gir kun aate, ni aado yaab aatiŋ ni. M ga li bi aa chee.”

ref: Jacob sent for Rachel and Leah to meet him in the pasture where he kept his sheep,
text: Nima pu na, Jakob nan tun ti bui Rachel ni Lea ke bi keln ni u waah bi kpaa tiwakor timoor ni nin chee na.

ref: and he told them: Your father isn't as friendly with me as he used to be, but the God my ancestors worshiped has been on my side.
text: Baah fuu ni na le u bui bi ke, “M bee ke nite aa ki gee maabɔr ke waah nan gee mi njan pu na; tɔ, nte Aawumbɔr bi m chee.

ref: years to buy your sheep and goats. During that time you kept changing my wages.
text: Nimi bilee bee, ni nyi ke m tun lituln sakpen tii nite.

ref: and that he keeps cheating me by changing my wages time after time. But God has protected me.
text: Le n‑yoonn pam, u jin mi pem pem ki kpeln waah ba ga pa mi pu na nfum kipiik pu. Uwumbɔr mu aa kii ke u bii mi.

ref: When your father said the speckled sheep would be my wages, all of them were speckled. And when he said the spotted ones would be mine, all of them were spotted.
text: N‑yoonn mu nite Labann ti bui ke, ‘Iŋoob i ye ichachal na ye min yee na’ kan, see i mɔmɔk ga maa kina ŋmeen la. U yaa ti ki bui ke, ‘Iŋoob i lul mbɔmbɔm ni mpepem na ye min yee na’ kan, see i mɔmɔk ga maa kina ŋmeen la.

ref: That's how God has taken sheep and goats from your father and given them to me.
text: Ni kan ke nite aah ji mi pem pem pu na, le Uwumbɔr di waapiih ni iŋoob di tii mi.

ref: Once, when the flocks were mating, I dreamed that all the rams were either spotted or speckled.
text: “Buyoonn tiwakor ngbaan nan piin ki bi maa na, le m daŋ tidaŋ, le ki kan ke iŋoja i nan bi dur ininkpel na nan ye i lul mbɔmbɔm ni mpepem na la.

ref: and he said, “Notice that all the rams are either spotted or speckled. I know everything Laban is doing to you,
text: Le u bui mi ke, ‘Lik, iŋoja i mɔmɔk bi dur ininkpel na ye ibɔnbɔn, ni ichachal la. Min le cha ni ŋani kina, ba pu? m kan Labann aah ŋani si pu na.

ref: and I am the God you worshiped at Bethel, when you poured olive oil on a rock and made a promise to me. Leave here at once and return to the land where you were born.”
text: Min le ye Uwumbɔr u nan dan aa chee Betel aatiŋ ni, nin chee aa nan di litakpal ŋum, ki di nkpan kpir li pu ki di tii mi, ke aa li teer mbɔr, ki nan ki puu tipuur tii mi na. Dandana wee, gor saawan mɔmɔk, ki gir buen baah nan ma si kitiŋ ki ponn ni na.’”

ref: He treats us like foreigners and has even cheated us out of the bride price that should have been ours.
text: U ŋani ti mu ke taah ye biwɔwɔm le na. U di timi kooh a, le dandana wee u jin tipar ngbaan doo a.

ref: So do whatever God tells you to do. Even the property God took from our father and gave to you really belongs to us and our children.
text: Le Uwumbɔr aah gaa waawankpal li ki di tii sin Jakob na, ye ti ni timi aabim yaan la. Uwumbɔr yaa bui ke aa ŋa pu na kan, ŋa kina.”

ref: Before Rachel left, she stole the household idols while Laban was out shearing his sheep.
text: Labann nan buen waawakor chee timoor ni, u ti chee waapiih aakor pu, le waah kaa bi buyoonn na, Rachel sun waawaa ŋi nan bi lichiln ni na.

ref: long years to earn your two daughters and another
text: Jakob nan ŋmann Labann, le kaa cha u nyi ke u ga nya udo.

ref: When Jacob crossed the Euphrates River and headed for the hill country of Gilead, he took with him everything he owned. Laban Catches Up with Jacob
text: U nan yoor waawan mɔmɔk le ki san mala buen. U nan ti puur Yufrates aamɔɔl, le ki piin ki cha ŋijoo aatiŋ ki bi yin ki ke Gilead na ni. Labann aah paan Jakob pu pu na aabɔr

ref: Three days later Laban found out that Jacob had gone.
text: Naah ti saa iwiin itaa daal na, le bi tuk Labann ke Jakob san a.

ref: So he took some of his relatives along and chased after Jacob for seven days, before catching up with him in the hill country of Gilead.
text: Le Labann yoor waajab ki paan Jakob pu ki ti saa iwiin ilole daal, le ki ti pii u ŋijoo aatiŋ Gilead ni.

ref: But God appeared to Laban in a dream that night and warned, “Don't say a word to Jacob. Don't make a threat or a promise.”
text: Kinyeek ngbaan le Uwumbɔr nan dan Labann chee tidaŋ ni, le ki bui u ke, “Aa li nyi ki taa ŋa Jakob nibaa.”

ref: Jacob had set up camp in the hill country of Gilead, when Laban and his relatives came and set up camp in another part of the hill country. Laban went to Jacob
text: Jakob ni waanib nan bi lijool libaa paab, le Labann mu ni waajab nan bi liken paab, Gilead aatiŋ ni.

ref: and said: Look what you've done! You've tricked me and run off with my daughters like a kidnapper.
text: Le Labann baa Jakob ke, “Ba ŋa aa ŋmann mi, ki yoor mbisatiib ke saah ton butɔb le ki gaa bi na?

ref: Why did you sneak away without telling me? I would have given you a going-away party with singing and with music on tambourines and harps.
text: Ba ŋa aa ŋmann mi ki loon san, kaa tuk mi? Aa yaa ba tuk mi kan, m ba ga cha m ni maanib yoor tiwangbaakaan le ki li gaa ilahn ki kpa mpopiin ki cheen nimi.

ref: You didn't even give me a chance to kiss my own grandchildren and daughters goodbye. That was really foolish.
text: Hali, saa cha m moor n‑yaabitiib ni mbisatiib aatakpin ki chɔi bi. Saah ŋa pu na, ni naahn kijɔrk aatuln la.

ref: I could easily hurt you, but the God your father worshiped has warned me not to make any threats or promises.
text: M ba kpa mpɔɔn ke m ŋa si bakaa, le kinyeek na, aate Aawumbɔr fe sur mi ke m li nyi m ŋa si bakaa ubaa.

ref: I can understand why you were eager to return to your father, but why did you have to steal my idols?
text: M bee ke aanimbil man aa gir kun, nima pu le aa san. Ba ŋa aa sun maadichal ni aawaa?”

ref: Jacob answered, “I left secretly because I was afraid you would take your daughters from me by force.
text: Jakob nan giin bui u ke, “M san ijawaan ki dak ke nibaakan aa ga gaa aabisatiib m chee la.

ref: If you find that any one of us has taken your idols, I'll have that person killed. Let your relatives be witnesses. Show me what belongs to you, and you can take it back.” Jacob did not realize that Rachel had stolen the household idols.
text: Aa mu yaa kan unii u joo saawaa na kan, ku udaan ngbaan. Ti mɔmɔk sɔ do, timi aanib bee le ga li ye biseeraadam, lik m ni maanib aawan mɔmɔk ponn ni, ki nyan saawan aah ye ni na mɔmɔk.” Jakob aa nan nyi ke upuu Rachel le sun ute Labann aawaa.

ref: Laban searched the tents of Jacob, Leah, and the two servant women, but did not find the idols. Then he went to Rachel's tent.
text: Le Labann nan koo Jakob aadiik ni ki biln, ki koo Lea mu, ni baanaagbiipiib bilee na aadir ponn ni ki biln, kaa kan waawaa ngbaan. Le u koo Rachel aadiik mu ni.

ref: She had already hidden them in the cushion she used as a saddle and was sitting on it. Laban searched everywhere and did not find them.
text: Rachel le nan yoor ŋiwaa ngbaan ki di bɔr laakumii aajal taab, waadiik ni, le ki kal li pu. Le Labann nan biln kidiik ponn ni, ni kookoo, kaa kan ŋi.

ref: Rachel said, “Father, please don't be angry with me for not getting up; I'm having my period.” Laban kept on searching, but still did not find the idols.
text: Rachel nan bui ute ke, “Uninkpel, taa gee liŋuul m pu, m fu tipiir la, nima pu le maan ŋmaa fii sil aanimbiin ni.” Le Labann nan biln, kaa kan waawaa ngbaan.

ref: Jacob became very angry and said to Laban: What have I done wrong? Have I committed some crime? Is that why you hunted me down?
text: Kina pu na, Jakob aasui nan fii, waa ki ŋmaa chuu ubaa, le ki baa Labann ke, “M tee ŋa si ba aabakaa. M tee bii nkaal mulamu aan aa kpa nsan ke aa paan ni m pu?

ref: After searching through everything I have, did you find anything of yours? If so, put it here, where your relatives and mine can see it. Then we can decide what to do.
text: Dandana wee aa biln timi aawan mɔmɔk ni, aa kan saawan nilani? Nyan ni siin, aan saanib ni maanib mɔmɔk kan ni, ki bee u ye mbamɔndaan na.

ref: If a wild animal killed one of your sheep or goats, I paid for it myself. In fact, you demanded the full price, whether the animal was killed during the day or at night.
text: N‑yoonn mumɔk tipeewanbir yaa nan ti chuu saawakork kibaa na kan, ni nan ye min le aafalaa. Maa nan yoor taakpab ti mɔk si ke naa ye maataani. N‑yoonn mumɔk unaayuk yaa nan ti sun saawakork kibaa kinyeek, bee nwiin pu na kan, see min le ga teen si.

ref: I sweated every day, and I couldn't sleep at night because of the cold.
text: N‑yoonn pam le nwiin nan gbaa mi, le tiwoor mu ŋun mi kinyeek, maa ŋmaa geeni.

ref: If the fearsome God worshiped by Abraham and my father Isaac had not been on my side, you would have sent me away without a thing. But God saw my hard work, and he knew the trouble I was in, so he helped me. Then last night he told you how wrong you were. Jacob and Laban Make an Agreement
text: Ni yaa kaa ye Uwumbɔr u nte Aisak ni n‑yaaja Abraham nan dii na nan bi m chee kan, kina m ba nyan aado nkuun la. Uwumbɔr aah kan maafalaa ngbaan, ni maah tun tii si pu na, le kinyeek na u fe bui si mbamɔn.” Jakob ni Labann aah puu tipuur tii tɔb pu na aabɔr

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “Leah and Rachel are my daughters, and their children belong to me. All these sheep you are taking are really mine too. In fact, everything you have belongs to me. But there is nothing I can do to keep my daughters and their children.
text: Labann nan bui Jakob ke, “Mpiibim bimina ye mbisatiib la, le min le yeh baabim ni tiwakor timina mɔmɔk. Mbamɔn, tiwan nimɔk bi do na ye maawan la. Le maah kaan ŋmaa gaa mbisatiib ni baabim na kan,

ref: So I am ready to make an agreement with you, and we will pile up some large rocks here to remind us of the agreement.”
text: m gor ke m ni si ŋa kimɔbaan. Cha ti kuun ŋitakpal bil nibaa chee aan ni li teer timi kimɔbaan ngbaan aabɔr.”

ref: he told his men to get some more rocks and pile them up next to it. Then Jacob and Laban ate a meal together beside the rocks.
text: U nan bui waanib ngbaan ke bi kuuni ŋitakpal ki nan di bil libuul libaa ni. Le bi nan kal ŋitakpal ngbaan chee ki jin tijikaar.

ref: Laban named the pile of rocks Jegar Sahadutha. But Jacob named it Galeed.
text: Labann aaliin ponn ni u nan yin nima chee ke, Jegar Sahadufa. Le Jakob mu aaliin ponn ni, u yin nima chee ke Galeed,

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “This pile of rocks will remind us of our agreement.” That's why the place was named Galeed.
text: Le Labann bui Jakob ke, “Ŋitakpal ŋi ti kuun bil na le ga cha ti li teer kimɔbaan ngbaan.” Nima le cha bi yin nima chee ke Galeed.

ref: Laban also said, “This pile of rocks means that the Lord will watch us both while we are apart from each other.” So the place was also named Mizpah.
text: Le Labann ki bui ke, “Uwumbɔr li lik ti mɔmɔk bilee, taah ga yakr tɔb chee pu na, ubaa taa ŋa uken bakaa.” Nima pu na, bi nan ki yin nima chee ke Mispa.

ref: Then Laban said: If you mistreat my daughters or marry other women, I may not know about it, but remember, God is watching us!
text: Labann nan ki bui ke, “Aa yaa ŋa mbisatiib bee bakaa, bee aa yaa ki yoor bipiib kpee bi pu kan, hali m yaa kaa nyi kan, teer ke Uwumbɔr waa si.

ref: Both this pile of rocks and this large rock have been set up between us as a reminder. I must never go past them to attack you, and you must never come past them to attack me.
text: Tiwan nilee ngbaan le ga li teer timi. M kii ke nwiin mbaa daal, maan lakr ŋitakpal ŋee pu jer ke m nan ban si kijaak, aa mu taa ŋa kina.

ref: My father Nahor, your grandfather Abraham, and their ancestors all worshiped the same God, and he will make sure that we each keep the agreement. Then Jacob made a promise in the name of the fearsome God his father Isaac had worshiped.
text: Aayaaja Abraham Aawumbɔr ni n‑yaaja Nahor Aawumbɔr le ga li ye timi aabɔjir.” Le Jakob aate Aisak Aawumbɔr aayimbil pu, u mu nan puu ke u mu ga li joo tipuur ngbaan.

ref: Jacob killed an animal and offered it as a sacrifice there on the mountain, and he invited his men to eat with him. After the meal they spent the night on the mountain.
text: U nan kɔr kiwakork ki toor kitork, lijool ngbaan paab, le ki nan chɔi waanib ngbaan, le bi dan nan jin tijikaar. Baah jin doo na, le bi mɔmɔk kpaan doon lijool ngbaan paab.

ref: Early the next morning, Laban kissed his daughters and his grandchildren goodbye, then he left to go back home.
text: Naah woln kichakpiik ni na, le Labann moor uyaabitiib ni ubisatiib aatakpin ki ŋa tinyoor ŋa bi pu, le ki chɔi bi, le ki gir kun udo.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Jakob mu nan cha, le Uwumbɔr aatuuntiib tooh u nsan ni.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Buyoonn u kan bi na, le u bui ke, “Uwumbɔr aatuuntiib sɔ,” nima le u nan yin nima chee ke Mahanaim.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: Le Jakob tun bitutum bi loln u pu nsan ki buen ukpel Esɔ chee, Edom aatiŋ ni.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: U nan bui bi ke bi ti bui Esɔ ke, “Min Jakob, saanaagbiija na, le ban m tuk maaninkpel Esɔ ke m buen Labann do ni yunn sakpen a, le dandana wee m ban ke m gir kun ni.

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”
text: M kpa inaa, ni ibon, ni ipiih, ni iŋoob, ni binaagbiib pam. Maaninkpel, nima pu le m tun ni ke bi nan tuk si, aa tii mi nsan m gir kun ni.”

ref: When the messengers returned, they told Jacob, “We went to your brother Esau, and now he is heading this way with
text: Buyoonn bitutum ngbaan gir ni Jakob chee na, bi nan bui u ke, “Ti buen aakpel Esɔ do, le u gor ki choo ke ni nan tooh tɔb nsan ni. U choo ki joo ni bijab ikui inaa la.”

ref: Jacob was so frightened that he divided his people, sheep, cattle, and camels into two groups.
text: Ijawaan nan chuu Jakob, le ni muk u. U nan chuu yakr binib bi bi u chee na nfum mulee, ki yakr waapiih, ni iŋoob, ni inaa, ni laakumii tiib mu kina.

ref: Then Jacob prayed: You, Lord, are the God who was worshiped by my grandfather Abraham and by my father Isaac. You told me to return home to my family, and you promised to be with me and make me successful.
text: Le Jakob mee Uwumbɔr ke, “N‑yaaja Abraham, ni nte Aisak Aawumbɔr, ŋun maameel. Uwumbɔr, sin le bui mi ke m gir kun maatiŋ, ni ndo yaab chee, ke aa ga li kii ki ter mi tiwan mɔmɔk ni.

ref: Please rescue me from my brother. I am afraid he will come and attack not only me, but my wives and children as well.
text: M mee si ke aa gaa mi nkpel Esɔ chee lii. M san ijawaan ke nibaakan u ni waanib choo bi nan ku ti mɔmɔk, hali ki ku bipiib ni mbim mu la.

ref: But you have promised that I would be a success and that someday it will be as hard to count my descendants as it is to count the grains of sand along the seashore.
text: Teer ke aa nan puu tipuur tii mi ke aa ga li kii ki ter mi tiwan mɔmɔk ni, ki ga cha m maa pɔr, le n‑yaabitiib ga li wiir ke litambol li dɔ limɔɔl aagbaan na, le ubaa aan ŋmaa kahn bi.”

ref: And Jacob told them to be sure to say that he was right behind them. Jacob hoped the gifts would make Esau friendly, so Esau would be glad to see him when they met.
text: See ni mɔmɔk bui u ke, ‘Een, saanaagbiija Jakob le bi kɔnkɔni do ki choo na.’” Jakob nan dakl ke, “Nibaakan Esɔ yaa kan ipiin ngbaan kan, u ga li gee mi, aan ti yaa kan tɔb kan, u ga di cha pinn mi.”

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Jakob nan bui ke, “M kan Uwumbɔr aanimbil wɔb le ki laa beenin fu.” Nima pu le u nan yin nima chee ke Peniel.

ref: Jacob put servants in charge of each herd and told them, “Go ahead of me and keep a space between each herd.”
text: U nan faa yakr ni ŋiker ŋiker, le ki cha waanaagbiib kpaa ŋiker ŋiker ngbaan nibaabaa. U nan bui bi ke, “Ni loln m pu nsan, aan ki li paa tɔb pu, ki li daa tɔb siib.”

ref: Then he said to the servant in charge of the first herd, “When Esau meets you, he will ask whose servant you are. He will want to know where you are going and who owns those animals in front of you.
text: U nan bui usaloln ke, “Nkpel Esɔ yaa ton si ki baa ke, ‘Saaninkpel ye ŋma? Aa cha la? Ŋma yeh tiwakor ngbaan?’ kan,

ref: So tell him, ‘They belong to your servant Jacob, who is coming this way. He is sending them as a gift to his master Esau.’ ”
text: aa bui u ke, ‘Saanaagbiija Jakob le yeh ti, le ki bui ke m di ti tii si, waaninkpel Esɔ. Jakob mu bi tipuwɔb ki choo dandana wee.’”

ref: Jacob also told the men in charge of the second and third herds and those who followed to say the same thing when they met Esau.
text: Jakob nan ki bui waanaagbiib bi paa nlee, ni ntaa, ni bimɔk gur na kina la: “Ni mɔmɔk bui Esɔ njan yoo aah ga bui u pu na.

ref: Jacob's men took the gifts on ahead of him, but he spent the night in camp. Jacob's Name Is Changed to Israel
text: U nan ki doon kinyeek ngbaan, waah yakr tiwakor nin chee na, le ki cha bi di ipiin ngbaan loln nsan buen Esɔ chee. Jakob ni Uwumbɔr aatuun aah nan mɔn Peniel aatiŋ ni pu na

ref: Jacob got up in the middle of the night and took his wives, his eleven children, and everything he owned across to the other side of the Jabbok River for safety.
text: Waah nan di bi puur doo na, le u ki di waawangol mɔmɔk mu di puur,

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: le u nan labr ti doon ubaa, lipepel liken na wɔb. Nima chee le Uwumbɔr aatuun dan kinyeek ngbaan, le bi nan mɔn kitaak ti ban ki woln.

ref: When the man saw that he could not win, he struck Jacob on the hip and threw it out of joint.
text: Uwumbɔr aatuun ngbaan aah kan ke waa ŋmaa nyaŋ u na, le u nyii Jakob aakpiik, le ki kper.

ref: They kept on wrestling until the man said, “Let go of me! It's almost daylight.” “You can't go until you bless me,” Jacob replied.
text: Le ntuun ngbaan bui u ke, “Ni ban ni woln la, di mi lii, aan m li cha.” Le Jakob bui u ke, “Maan cha aa buen, see aa ŋa tinyoor ŋa m pu.”

ref: Then the man asked, “What is your name?” “Jacob,” he answered.
text: Ntuun ngbaan nan baa u ke, “Bi yin si ke ba?” Le u bui ke, “Jakob.”

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Le ntuun ngbaan bui ke, “Saah mɔn ki nyaŋ Uwumbɔr ni unibɔn na, nima pu na, bi ga li gur yin si ke Israel la, baan ki yin si ke Jakob.”

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Le Jakob bui ke, “Aa mu tuk mi saayimbil.” Le u nan baa u ke, “Ba ŋa aa ban ke aa bee maayimbil?” Le u ŋa tinyoor ŋa Jakob pu.

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Le nwiin aah puu buyoonn na Jakob nyan Peniel ki cha, le ki nan deeni ukpiik pu.

ref: and the muscle on his hip joint had been injured. That's why even today the people of Israel don't eat the hip muscle of any animal.
text: Hali, ki yoor ni Jakob aayoonn na ki nan saa din, Israel yaab aa ji kikpiik pu aanann; ba pu? Jakob aakpiik pu le Uwumbɔr aatuun ngbaan nan nyii.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Jakob mu nan cha, le Uwumbɔr aatuuntiib tooh u nsan ni.

ref: But Esau ran toward Jacob and hugged and kissed him. Then the two brothers started crying.
